161.upsc: Tests the Space Race context, the Cold War competition with the Soviet Union, and how the Moon landing inspired India's space programme. Vikram Sarabhai, who founded ISRO, died in 1971 before seeing India's own achievements.
161.remember: Armstrong's famous quote: 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind', the 'a' was lost in transmission.

162.label: Vietnam War ended
162.what: The Vietnam War ended on 30 April 1975 when North Vietnamese forces captured Saigon, reunifying Vietnam under communist rule. The USA lost 58,000 soldiers and the war cost over 100 billion dollars.
162.upsc: Tests India's position (supported North Vietnam diplomatically while maintaining ties with USA), the domino theory, and how Vietnam's defeat of a superpower influenced liberation movements worldwide.
162.remember: The last US personnel fled Saigon by helicopter from the embassy roof as the city fell. The iconic photograph of the evacuation became a symbol of American defeat. Vietnam was later the fastest growing economy in Asia.

163.label: Berlin Wall
163.what: The Berlin Wall fell (1989), leading to German reunification (1990) and the collapse of communist regimes across Eastern Europe.
163.upsc: Tests the Cold War context, German reunification, and how the fall of the Wall triggered the collapse of communist regimes across Eastern Europe and ultimately the Soviet Union in 1991.
163.remember: The wall actually fell because of a mistake; a government official accidentally announced that border crossings were open 'immediately,' and the crowds just took over.

164.label: Soviet Union
164.what: The Soviet Union formally dissolved on 25 December 1991 when Mikhail Gorbachev resigned and the USSR ceased to exist. Fifteen independent republics emerged, ending the Cold War that had dominated world politics since 1945.
164.upsc: Tests reasons (economic stagnation, Gorbachev's Glasnost/Perestroika) and the impact on India's 'pro-Soviet' foreign policy.
164.remember: The mightiest military empire in history collapsed without a shot being fired, essentially ending in a series of press conferences.

165.label: World Trade Organization
165.what: The World Trade Organization (WTO) (est. 1995) replaced GATT to provide a global framework for trade rules and disputes.
165.upsc: Tests the 'TRIPS' agreement and India's role in protecting its generic medicine industry from Western patent laws.
165.remember: India has used WTO rules to ensure it can produce life-saving drugs cheaply, a major win for public health in developing nations.

166.label: 9/11 attacks on USA
166.what: The 9/11 Attacks (2001) saw Al-Qaeda hijack planes to hit the Twin Towers and Pentagon, killing nearly 3,000 people.
166.upsc: Tests the US 'War on Terror', invasion of Afghanistan, and the transformation of India-US strategic relations.
166.remember: The attacks led to the 'Department of Homeland Security' and changed global aviation and security protocols forever.

167.label: Global Financial Crisis
167.what: The 2008 Global Financial Crisis was triggered by the US housing bubble and the collapse of Lehman Brothers.
167.upsc: Tests the causes (deregulation) and why India remained relatively insulated due to conservative RBI banking rules.
167.remember: India emerged from the 2008 crisis relatively unscathed because the RBI had maintained conservative banking regulations. Governor Y.V. Reddy who had resisted financial deregulation was later credited with saving India.

168.label: Paris Climate Agreement
168.what: The Paris Agreement was adopted under the UNFCCC in December 2015. It aims to hold the increase in global average temperature to well below 2°C above pre-industrial levels while pursuing efforts to limit it to 1.5°C, and it entered into force in 2016.
168.upsc: Tests India's 'Net Zero 2070' commitment and the principle of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities' (CBDR).
168.remember: India argues that rich nations caused the problem and should pay for the transition; India's 2070 target gives it time to develop.

169.label: ISRO
169.what: The Indian Space Research Organisation was established on 15 August 1969 under the visionary physicist Vikram Sarabhai, who believed space technology could solve India's developmental problems.
169.upsc: Tests ISRO's philosophy and its evolution from transport by bicycle to a global leader in low-cost satellite launches.
169.remember: India's first rocket parts were transported on a bicycle; today ISRO launches satellites for dozens of other nations.

170.label: India's first satellite Aryabhata
170.what: Aryabhata (1975) was India's first satellite, launched by a Soviet rocket. It studied X-rays and solar physics.
170.upsc: Tests that India did not launch Aryabhata itself but relied on a Soviet rocket, marking India's entry into the space age. UPSC traces India's growing launch independence from 1975 onward.
170.remember: Although it failed after four days in orbit, Aryabhata proved India could build complex satellites indigenously.

171.label: India's first indigenous satellite APPLE
171.what: APPLE (Ariane Passenger Payload Experiment) was India's first experimental geostationary communication satellite, launched in 1981 by the European Ariane rocket. It demonstrated India's ability to build communication satellites.
171.upsc: Tests the sequence of India's satellite development: Aryabhata (1975), Bhaskara (1979), APPLE (1981), INSAT-1 (1982). APPLE proved the technology used in INSAT satellites.
171.remember: During testing, the satellite was famously transported on a bullock cart to avoid vibration, an iconic image of Indian science.

172.label: India's first SLV-3 rocket launch
172.what: India's first Satellite Launch Vehicle SLV-3 successfully placed the Rohini satellite into orbit on 18 July 1980. The mission was led by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam, making India one of six nations with indigenous launch capability.
172.upsc: Tests Abdul Kalam's role, the significance of indigenous launch capability, and the failed first attempt in 1979. SLV-3 was the foundation for India's later ASLV and PSLV programmes.
172.remember: After the 1979 test failed, Kalam took responsibility; the 1980 success made India the 6th nation with launch capability.

173.label: PSLV first successful flight
173.what: The PSLV (successful since 1994) is ISRO's 'workhorse' rocket, known for its high reliability and multiple satellite launches.
173.upsc: PSLV is the most tested ISRO rocket. Tests its success rate, payload capacity, and landmark missions: Chandrayaan-1 (2008), Mangalyaan (2013), and the record 104 satellites in one launch (2017).
173.remember: The PSLV has had over 50 successful flights, becoming one of the most reliable rockets in the world today.

174.label: Chandrayaan-1
174.what: Chandrayaan-1 (2008) was India's first lunar mission. It famously discovered water molecules on the Moon's surface.
174.upsc: Tests the water discovery, the Moon Impact Probe that carried the Indian flag, and that NASA's instrument on board made the water detection. The mission ended in 2009 when contact was lost.
174.remember: India 'planted its flag' by crashing the Moon Impact Probe, painted with the tricolour, into the lunar south pole.

175.label: Mangalyaan (Mars Orbiter Mission)
175.what: Mangalyaan, the Mars Orbiter Mission, was launched in November 2013 and entered Mars orbit on 24 September 2014. India became the first country to reach Mars on its first attempt, at a cost of Rs. 450 crore.
175.upsc: Tests the mission's low cost (cheaper than the movie 'Gravity') and its success in studying the Martian atmosphere.
175.remember: ISRO scientists celebrated the orbit entry at 7:41 AM on 24 Sep; the mission lasted far longer than its 6-month design.

176.label: GSLV Mk-III first flight
176.what: The GSLV Mk-III (now LVM3) completed its first developmental flight in June 2017. It is India's heaviest rocket, capable of placing 4-tonne satellites into geostationary orbit, ending dependence on foreign rockets for heavy payloads.
176.upsc: Tests the distinction between PSLV, GSLV and GSLV Mk-III. Mk-III uses India's own cryogenic engine, ending dependence on Russia for cryogenic technology. Used for Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3.
176.remember: India struggled for decades to develop cryogenic engine technology after the USA pressured Russia not to transfer it in the 1990s. Mk-III represents India's indigenous solution after 20 years of effort.

177.label: Chandrayaan-3
177.sublabel: first soft landing near Moon's south pole
177.what: Chandrayaan-3 landed the Vikram lander near the Moon's south pole on 23 August 2023, making India the first country to land near the lunar south pole and the fourth to soft-land on the Moon.
177.upsc: Tests the Pragyan rover, the landing site named Shiv Shakti Point, the significance of south pole water ice deposits, and that Chandrayaan-2 had failed to land in 2019.
177.remember: India landed just days after Russia's Luna-25 crashed attempting the same feat. The south pole matters because of confirmed water ice, which could support future human settlements on the Moon.

178.label: Aditya-L1 solar mission
178.what: Aditya-L1 (2023) is India's first solar mission, positioned at the Lagrange Point 1 to study the Sun continuously.
178.upsc: Tests the concept of Lagrange points, Aditya-L1's scientific objectives (studying solar wind, corona, and solar flares), and its significance as India's first dedicated solar mission.
178.remember: L1 is a 'gravitational sweet spot' 1.5 million km from Earth, allowing uninterrupted solar observation without eclipses.

179.label: India's first nuclear reactor Apsara
179.what: Apsara (1956) was India's (and Asia's) first nuclear reactor, designed by Homi Bhabha and built at Trombay.
179.upsc: Tests Bhabha's 'Three-Stage Nuclear Power Programme' and the early push for nuclear self-sufficiency.
179.remember: Nehru suggested the name 'Apsara' (nymph); the reactor was critical for producing medical and industrial isotopes.

180.label: India's first computer TIFRAC
180.what: TIFRAC (Tata Institute of Fundamental Research Automatic Calculator) became operational in 1960 at TIFR Mumbai. It was one of the first computers in Asia and the first computer designed and built in India.
180.upsc: Tests India's early computing history. UPSC may ask about the transition from TIFRAC to modern computing, C-DAC's development of the Param supercomputer in 1991, and India's IT policy evolution.
180.remember: TIFRAC was built by a team led by R. Narasimhan. It took 5 years to build. The machine weighed several tonnes and occupied an entire room. India's IT journey from TIFRAC to a global software power took 60 years.

181.label: India's Green Revolution wheat variety
181.what: The Green Revolution from 1966 introduced High Yielding Varieties of wheat and rice developed by Norman Borlaug and adapted by M.S. Swaminathan, transforming India from a food-deficit to a food-surplus nation.
181.upsc: Tests M.S. Swaminathan's role as father of Green Revolution in India, the states most affected (Punjab, Haryana, western UP), and the long-term problems: groundwater depletion, soil degradation, regional inequality.
181.remember: It helped India escape famine-era vulnerability, but the later bill showed up in groundwater stress, chemical dependence, and regional imbalance.

182.label: Operation Flood (White Revolution)
182.what: Operation Flood (1970) led by Verghese Kurien made India the world's largest milk producer via the 'Amul' model.
182.upsc: Tests Verghese Kurien as the Milkman of India, the Amul cooperative model in Anand Gujarat, the three phases of Operation Flood (1970-1996), and India's current status as top milk producer.
182.remember: Amul started with just two village cooperatives; today it is a multi-billion dollar brand owned by 3.6 million farmers.

183.label: Raja Ram Mohan Roy
183.what: Raja Ram Mohan Roy (1772-1833) was a Bengali polymath, social reformer and founder of the Brahmo Samaj. He campaigned against Sati, advocated English education and is called the Father of the Indian Renaissance.
183.upsc: Tests his campaign against Sati (abolished 1829), promotion of English education, and his monotheistic reforms.
183.remember: Roy was a master of languages; he published newspapers in Bengali, Persian, and English to spread reformist ideas.

184.label: Swami Dayananda Saraswati
184.what: Swami Dayananda Saraswati founded the Arya Samaj (1875), advocating 'Back to the Vedas' and rejecting idol worship.
184.upsc: Tests his book 'Satyarth Prakash' and the 'Shuddhi' movement for reconversion to Hinduism.
184.remember: Arya Samaj founded the DAV (Dayananda Anglo-Vedic) schools, which blended modern science with Vedic teaching.

185.label: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa
185.what: Ramakrishna Paramahamsa was a mystic who taught the essential unity of all religions: 'As many faiths, so many paths'.
185.upsc: Tests his influence on Swami Vivekananda and the founding of the Ramakrishna Mission after his death.
185.remember: Ramakrishna practiced Islam and Christianity for short periods to experience their truths firsthand.

186.label: Rabindranath Tagore
186.what: Rabindranath Tagore (1861-1941) was a Bengali polymath who composed the national anthems of India (Jana Gana Mana) and Bangladesh (Amar Shonar Bangla) and became the first non-European Nobel laureate in literature in 1913.
186.upsc: Tests his Nobel Prize for 'Gitanjali' (1913) and his return of the knighthood after Jallianwala Bagh (1919).
186.remember: Tagore wrote both India's and Bangladesh's national anthems. He also wrote the song that inspired Sri Lanka's national anthem. No other person has written national anthems for multiple countries.

187.label: Bal Gangadhar Tilak
187.what: Bal Gangadhar Tilak (1856-1920) was a firebrand nationalist who popularised mass mobilisation through Ganesh Chaturthi and Shivaji festivals. He declared Swaraj is my birthright and I shall have it.
187.upsc: Tests the 'Lal-Bal-Pal' trio, the 'Home Rule League', and his use of Ganesh Chaturthi for mass mobilisation.
187.remember: Tilak started Ganesh Chaturthi as a public festival in 1893 to bring communities together for nationalist activities under the cover of religion. It became the template for mass mobilisation.

188.label: Bipin Chandra Pal
188.what: Bipin Chandra Pal was the 'Father of Revolutionary Thought' in India, part of the Extremist trio Lal-Bal-Pal.
188.upsc: Tests his advocacy for 'Swadeshi' and total independence long before it became the Congress's official goal.
188.remember: Pal was one of the fiercest public speakers of his generation. He helped make nationalism sound bold, impatient, and mass-facing.

189.label: Swami Vivekananda
189.what: Swami Vivekananda (1863–1902) introduced Vedanta and Yoga to the West, founding the Ramakrishna Mission in 1897.
189.upsc: Tests the Chicago speech, the Ramakrishna Mission, his philosophy of practical Vedanta, and his influence on nationalism. Netaji Bose and many revolutionaries cited Vivekananda as their inspiration.
189.remember: Vivekananda predicted he would not live to 40; he died at 39, having 'done the work of 300 years' in just 15.

190.label: Swami Vivekananda's Chicago speech
190.what: Swami Vivekananda's speech at the Parliament of the World's Religions in Chicago on 11 September 1893, beginning with 'Sisters and Brothers of America', brought him international recognition and introduced many Western audiences to Vedanta and Hindu thought.
190.upsc: Tests the impact of the speech on India's global image and the founding of Vedanta societies in the West.
190.remember: He began with 'Sisters and Brothers of America', a greeting that resonated deeply with his Western audience.

191.label: Mahatma Gandhi
191.what: Mahatma Gandhi led India's independence movement through Satyagraha (non-violent resistance), pioneering techniques like fasting, hartals and non-cooperation that influenced civil rights movements worldwide.
191.upsc: One of the most tested UPSC figures. Know his South Africa years, Champaran Satyagraha (1917), Non-Cooperation Movement (1920), Dandi March (1930), Quit India (1942), and assassination on 30 January 1948.
191.remember: Gandhi was nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize five times but never won. The committee later admitted this was a 'great omission'.

192.label: Lala Lajpat Rai
192.what: Lala Lajpat Rai (1865–1928), the 'Lion of Punjab', died from injuries sustained during a protest against the Simon Commission.
192.upsc: Tests his role in the Lal-Bal-Pal trio, founding the Punjab National Bank, and his influence on Bhagat Singh.
192.remember: Lajpat Rai famously declared: 'Every blow struck on my body will be the last nail in the coffin of British imperialism.'

193.label: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
193.what: Sardar Patel (1875–1950) was the 'Iron Man of India' who integrated 562 princely states into the Indian Union.
193.upsc: Tests his role as Deputy PM, integration of Hyderabad/Junagadh, and his status as the 'Patron Saint of Civil Servants'.
193.remember: Patel integrated 562 princely states in under a year, an act of political consolidation with no parallel in history. He said he used the same technique as Bismarck: blood and iron, though he preferred negotiation.

194.label: Muhammad Ali Jinnah
194.what: Muhammad Ali Jinnah (1876-1948) was a major political leader of late colonial India who moved from being associated with Hindu-Muslim unity to becoming the central political force behind the demand for Pakistan.
194.upsc: Tests his evolution from 'Ambassador of Unity' to the 1940 Lahore Resolution and the demand for Partition.
194.remember: Jinnah was a secular-minded lawyer who rarely practiced religious rituals, yet he led the movement for a state based on Islamic identity.

195.label: Jawaharlal Nehru
195.what: Jawaharlal Nehru (1889–1964) was India's first PM, architect of the modern state, and a leader of the Non-Aligned Movement.
195.upsc: Tests his socialist model, Five-Year Plans, and the failure of the 1962 war which damaged his legacy.
195.remember: Nehru's 'Tryst with Destiny' speech is considered one of the 20th century's most iconic pieces of political oratory.

196.label: B.R. Ambedkar
196.what: B.R. Ambedkar (1891-1956) was the principal architect of India's Constitution and one of the foremost leaders of Dalit rights. He converted to Buddhism in 1956 in a mass ceremony at Nagpur shortly before his death.
196.upsc: Tests his role in the Drafting Committee, the Poona Pact, and his mass conversion to Buddhism in 1956.
196.remember: Ambedkar had three doctorates from Columbia and LSE; he was the most educated Indian leader of his time.

197.label: Subhas Chandra Bose
197.what: Subhas Chandra Bose (1897–1945) led the INA and the Azad Hind government, seeking independence through armed struggle.
197.upsc: Tests the INA, the Azad Hind Fauj, the Red Fort trials, Bose's split with Gandhi over methods, his escape from India and journey to Germany and Japan, and the circumstances of his reported death.
197.remember: Bose's 'Rani of Jhansi Regiment' was the first all-women combat unit in Asia, breaking deep gender taboos.

198.label: Bhagat Singh
198.what: Bhagat Singh (1907–1931) was a revolutionary socialist who was hanged at 23 for the Lahore Conspiracy Case.
198.upsc: Tests his 'HSRA' role, his pamphlet 'Why I am an Atheist', and his push for political prisoner status for revolutionaries.
198.remember: He spent his last night in jail reading a book by Lenin. He asked his executioners to let him finish the chapter before they took him.

199.label: APJ Abdul Kalam
199.what: A.P.J. Abdul Kalam (1931-2015) was the Missile Man of India who led the SLV-3 and Agni missile programmes, served as President from 2002-2007, and became the most beloved president in India's modern history.
199.upsc: Tests his role in SLV-3 and the Agni missiles, and his focus on youth and 'Vision 2020'.
199.remember: Kalam was a vegetarian who played the veena, wrote Tamil poetry and slept only four hours a night. He owned nothing when he died: his possessions were a few books, some clothes, a laptop and a CD player.

200.label: Rabindranath Tagore won Nobel Prize
200.what: Tagore's Nobel Prize (1913) for 'Gitanjali' made him the first Asian and first non-European to win in literature.
200.upsc: Tests the impact on Indian pride and Tagore's return of his knighthood in 1919 in protest of Jallianwala Bagh.
200.remember: W.B. Yeats wrote the famous introduction to the English translation of 'Gitanjali', helping it gain global fame.

201.label: Mother Teresa received Nobel Peace Prize
201.what: Mother Teresa (1910–1997) won the Nobel Peace Prize (1979) for her work with the poor in Calcutta via the Missionaries of Charity.
201.upsc: Tests her Indian citizenship (1948), her Bharat Ratna (1980), and her canonisation as 'Saint Teresa' in 2016.
201.remember: Teresa donated the entire prize money, and the cost of the formal banquet, directly to her work in the slums.

202.label: Amartya Sen won Nobel Prize in Economics
202.what: Amartya Sen won the Nobel Prize in Economics in 1998 for contributions to welfare economics, social choice theory, and his analysis showing famines result from policy failure and inequality, not food shortage.
202.upsc: Know his 'Capability Approach' and his finding that no functioning democracy has ever had a major famine because a free press and elections force leaders to act.
202.remember: Sen proved that famines almost never happen in functioning democracies because a free press and elections force governments to act. The 1943 Bengal famine killed 2 to 3 million people while food was being exported from India.

203.label: Brahmo Samaj
203.sublabel: founded by Raja Ram Mohan Roy
203.what: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded the Brahmo Samaj in Calcutta in 1828, a monotheistic reform movement that rejected idol worship, caste discrimination and advocated for women's rights and English education.
203.upsc: Tests his role in the 1829 Sati ban and his advocacy for English education and women's property rights.
203.remember: Roy was the first Indian to use the press for social reform, publishing newspapers in Bengali, Persian, and English.

204.label: Sati Abolition Act
204.what: Lord William Bentinck passed the Sati Regulation (1829), making the practice a criminal offence, backed by Raja Ram Mohan Roy.
204.upsc: Tests Bentinck's role, Raja Ram Mohan Roy's campaign that enabled it, and the orthodox Hindu opposition led by Radha Kanta Deb. UPSC connects this to the broader reform vs. tradition debate.
204.remember: Roy personally went to London to argue against the orthodox appeal to restore Sati, he died there in 1833.

205.label: Widow Remarriage Act
205.what: The Hindu Widows Remarriage Act of 1856 legalised the remarriage of Hindu widows, largely due to the persistent campaigning of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar who collected thousands of signatures.
205.upsc: Tests Vidyasagar's role and the social context. Child widows were common due to child marriage. The Act faced fierce opposition from orthodox Hindus but was a landmark in women's rights.
205.remember: Vidyasagar personally arranged and financed the first widow remarriage; he endured social boycott and death threats.

206.label: Arya Samaj
206.what: Swami Dayananda Saraswati (1824–1883) founded the Arya Samaj (1875), rejecting idol worship and caste based on birth.
206.upsc: Tests the 'Shuddhi' movement and the 'Back to the Vedas' slogan. He wrote 'Satyarth Prakash'.
206.remember: Unlike the Brahmo Samaj, the Arya Samaj sought reform entirely from within the authority of the Vedas.

207.label: Aligarh Muslim University
207.sublabel: founded by Sir Syed Ahmed Khan
207.what: Sir Syed Ahmed Khan founded the Aligarh Movement and the M.A.O. College (1875) to promote modern education for Muslims.
207.upsc: Tests his belief that Muslims had to embrace English education to progress and his eventual 'Two-Nation' stance.
207.remember: The M.A.O. College became Aligarh Muslim University in 1920; it remains a center of modern Islamic thought.

208.label: Ramakrishna Mission
208.what: Swami Vivekananda founded the Ramakrishna Mission (1897) for spiritual and social service, based on his guru's teachings.
208.upsc: Tests Vivekananda's 1893 Chicago speech at the Parliament of World Religions which brought international attention to Hinduism. UPSC connects his ideas to religious nationalism and social reform.
208.remember: Vivekananda was the first monk to cross the oceans; he broke the 'Kala Pani' taboo to represent Hinduism abroad.

209.label: Servants of India Society
209.what: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded the Servants of India Society in 1905, training Indians to dedicate their lives to public service through selfless work across education, healthcare and social causes.
209.upsc: Tests his role as Gandhi's political mentor and his moderate approach versus Tilak's extremism.
209.remember: Gandhi spent a year traveling through India after his return in 1915, following Gokhale's advice to 'know the land first'.

210.label: Self-Respect Movement
210.what: E.V. Ramasamy, known as Periyar, founded the Self-Respect Movement in 1925 in Tamil Nadu to fight Brahmin dominance, caste oppression and superstition, advocating rational thinking and Dravidian identity.
210.upsc: Tests Periyar's role in the Dravidian movement, his fierce opposition to Hindi imposition, advocacy for women's rights and inter-caste marriages, and his influence on Tamil Nadu politics.
210.remember: Periyar organised Self-Respect marriages: simple ceremonies without priests, rituals, or dowries. He also led the 1965 anti-Hindi agitation that forced the central government to keep English as an official language.

211.label: Ambedkar's Mahad Satyagraha
211.sublabel: Dalits claimed right to public water
211.what: The Mahad Satyagraha (1927) was Ambedkar's first major civil rights action, demanding Dalit access to the Chawdar tank.
211.upsc: The Mahad Satyagraha is considered the start of Ambedkar's civil rights movement. Tests his methods, the burning of Manusmriti at the event, and its significance as India's first Dalit civil rights action.
211.remember: Ambedkar compared the Mahad struggle to the French Revolution, calling it a fight for 'Equality, Liberty and Fraternity'.

212.label: Devadasi system abolished
212.what: The Devadasi system (temple sexual servitude) was abolished in Madras (1947) through the efforts of Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddi.
212.upsc: UPSC tests the social reform context, Dr. Muthulakshmi Reddi's campaign, and the intersection of religion, caste, and gender in colonial and early post-colonial reform.
212.remember: The reform debate was difficult because defenders called it tradition, while reformers pointed out the exploitation hiding inside that tradition.

213.label: Hindu Code Bills
213.what: The Hindu Code Bills (1955–56) reformed Hindu personal law, giving women equal rights in marriage, divorce, and inheritance.
213.upsc: Tests Ambedkar's role as Law Minister and his resignation when the comprehensive bill was initially delayed.
213.remember: Ambedkar resigned saying Nehru lacked the courage to pass the full bill; it was later passed as four separate acts.

214.label: Untouchability Offences Act
214.what: The Untouchability Offences Act (1955) criminalised the practice of untouchability, implementing Article 17 of the Constitution.
214.upsc: Tests the difference between the 1955 Act and the later, stronger 1989 SC/ST Prevention of Atrocities Act.
214.remember: Article 17 is the only Fundamental Right that is 'absolute', it cannot be restricted under any circumstances.

215.label: Mandal Commission report
215.what: The Mandal Commission (1980) recommended 27% reservation for OBCs in govt jobs, implemented by V.P. Singh in 1990.
215.upsc: One of the most socially significant decisions in post-independence India. Tests the Indra Sawhney case (1992) which upheld OBC reservations but capped total reservations at 50 percent.
215.remember: The implementation triggered massive protests by upper-caste students and fundamentally changed Indian electoral politics.

216.label: Indian Independence Act
216.what: The Indian Independence Act (1947) created the Dominions of India and Pakistan, abolishing the post of Secretary of State.
216.upsc: Tests the legal mechanism of independence. India remained a 'Dominion' until it became a Republic in 1950.
216.remember: The King of England remained the formal head of state of India until 26 January 1950, represented by the Governor-General.

217.label: Constituent Assembly first met
217.what: The Constituent Assembly first met on 9 Dec 1946. Its strength was reduced from 389 to 299 after Partition.
217.upsc: Tests key figures (Sinha, Prasad, Ambedkar) and the 11 sessions held over 2 years, 11 months, and 18 days.
217.remember: Only 284 members were present on 24 Jan 1950 to sign the final calligraphed copies of the Constitution.

218.label: B.R. Ambedkar became Law Minister
218.what: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was appointed Law Minister in Nehru's first cabinet and chaired the seven-member Drafting Committee, making him the principal architect of the Constitution.
218.upsc: One of the most tested UPSC personalities. Tests his role in the Drafting Committee, inclusion of Fundamental Rights, his conversion to Buddhism in 1956, and his resignation over the Hindu Code Bill.
218.remember: Ambedkar called Article 32 (Right to Constitutional Remedies) the Soul of the Constitution, saying rights without enforcement are meaningless. He resigned in 1951 saying Nehru lacked the courage to pass the Hindu Code Bill.

219.label: 42nd Constitutional Amendment
219.sublabel: Mini Constitution of the Emergency era
219.what: The 42nd Amendment (1976), the 'Mini-Constitution', added 'Socialist, Secular, Integrity' to the Preamble and curtailed the courts.
219.upsc: Tests the addition of 'Fundamental Duties' (Art 51A) and the sweeping powers given to the executive during the Emergency.
219.remember: This was the only time the Preamble was ever amended; the changes were made while many opposition MPs were in jail.

220.label: 44th Constitutional Amendment
220.what: The 44th Amendment (1978) restored many rights, reclassifying the 'Right to Property' as a legal right under Article 300A.
220.upsc: Tests the safeguards added against Emergency abuse (Cabinet written advice, 'Armed Rebellion' term).
220.remember: It ensured that Article 21 (Life and Liberty) cannot be suspended even during an emergency, a direct result of 1975 lessons.

221.label: 52nd Amendment: Anti-Defection Law
221.what: The 52nd Amendment (1985) introduced the 'Anti-Defection Law' via the Tenth Schedule to stop 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' politics.
221.upsc: Tests the role of the Speaker in disqualifying members and the 2003 amendment (91st) that removed 'splits' as an exception.
221.remember: A member is disqualified if they voluntarily give up party membership or vote against their party's 'Whip' in the house.

222.label: 73rd Amendment: Panchayati Raj
222.what: The 73rd Amendment (1992) gave constitutional status to Panchayati Raj, adding the Eleventh Schedule with 29 subjects.
222.upsc: Tests the three-tier structure, reservation for women (33%), and State Finance/Election Commissions.
222.remember: Before 1992, Panchayats were only a Directive Principle (Art 40); the amendment made them a mandatory third tier of govt.

223.label: 74th Amendment: Urban Local Bodies
223.what: The 74th Amendment (1992) granted constitutional status to Urban Local Bodies (Municipalities), adding the Twelfth Schedule.
223.upsc: Often tested alongside the 73rd Amendment. Know the Twelfth Schedule, types of urban local bodies, reservation provisions, and State Election and Finance Commissions.
223.remember: Like the 73rd, it ensures direct elections and fixed five-year terms for urban local governments.

224.label: 86th Amendment: Right to Education
224.what: The 86th Amendment of 2002 inserted Article 21A making free and compulsory education a Fundamental Right for children aged 6 to 14. It also added a Fundamental Duty for parents to provide education.
224.upsc: This amendment enabled the Right to Education Act 2009. UPSC tests Article 21A, the age group, and the distinction between the constitutional amendment and the implementing legislation.
224.remember: Education for children below 6 remains a Directive Principle (Art 45), while 6–14 is a legally enforceable Fundamental Right.

225.label: Right to Education Act
225.what: The Right to Education Act 2009 implemented Article 21A, mandating free and compulsory education for all children aged 6 to 14. It required private schools to reserve 25 percent seats for economically weaker sections.
225.upsc: Tests the 25 percent reservation requirement for private schools, the no-detention policy (later amended), and the role of neighbourhood schools. The Society for Unaided Private Schools case upheld the 25 percent rule.
225.remember: The Supreme Court upheld the 25% reservation for EWS, calling it a 'reasonable restriction' on the rights of private schools.

226.label: National Food Security Act
226.what: The National Food Security Act (2013) legally entitles 67% of India's population to subsidised food grains.
226.upsc: Tests the coverage (75% rural, 50% urban) and the prices: Rice ₹3, Wheat ₹2, Coarse grains ₹1 per kg.
226.remember: The act also provides for a maternity benefit of ₹6,000 for pregnant women, a key part of India's nutritional safety net.

227.label: Junagadh accession to India
227.what: The Nawab of Junagadh signed accession to Pakistan despite a Hindu-majority population. After the Nawab fled to Pakistan, India held a referendum in which 99% voted to join India.
227.upsc: Tests the three difficult integrations alongside Hyderabad and Kashmir. Junagadh is the only one resolved by referendum. Pakistan still claims Junagadh legally as the original accession was to Pakistan.
227.remember: Pakistan technically still has a government-in-exile for Junagadh. It is a forgotten irony that Pakistan claims Junagadh by the same accession logic India uses to claim Kashmir.

228.label: Nehru's Tryst with Destiny speech
228.what: Nehru delivered his Tryst with Destiny speech to the Constituent Assembly at the stroke of midnight on 14-15 August 1947, marking India's independence from British rule.
228.upsc: Tests the exact date and occasion of the speech, the phrase at the stroke of the midnight hour, and its significance as the founding oratorical moment of the Indian republic.
228.remember: The speech was broadcast live on All India Radio. Nehru wrote it himself the night before. It is considered one of the greatest speeches in history and is still studied in political science curricula worldwide.

229.label: First Republic Day parade
229.what: The first Republic Day parade was held on 26 January 1950 on Rajpath (now Kartavya Path) in New Delhi, marking the commencement of the Constitution and India becoming a republic.
229.upsc: Tests the distinction between Independence Day (15 Aug) and Republic Day (26 Jan), the significance of 26 January (linked to 1930 Purna Swaraj), and the parade as a symbol of military and cultural diversity.
229.remember: The Chief Guest at the first Republic Day was Indonesian President Sukarno. The tradition of inviting a foreign head of state as Chief Guest began from day one and continues today.

230.label: Hindi declared official language
230.what: When the Constitution came into force in 1950, Article 343 declared Hindi in Devanagari script as the official language of the Union, with English to continue for 15 years as a transitional measure.
230.upsc: Tests Article 343-351 (Official Language provisions), the Official Languages Act 1963 which kept English indefinitely, and the 1965 anti-Hindi agitations in Tamil Nadu that forced the government to retain English.
230.remember: The 15-year deadline for switching fully to Hindi expired in 1965. Tamil Nadu erupted in violent protests. Self-immolations occurred. The government backed down and retained English. Hindi is official but not the sole language of administration.

231.label: First Five-Year Plan completed
231.what: India's First Five Year Plan (1951-1956) concluded having exceeded its growth target of 2.1%, achieving 3.6% GDP growth. It focused on agriculture, irrigation, and power generation.
231.upsc: Tests the Harrod-Domar model used for the First Plan, the focus areas, and the contrast with the Second Plan (Mahalanobis model, heavy industry). Bhakra-Nangal dam was a flagship project.
231.remember: Nehru called dams the temples of modern India. The Bhakra-Nangal project, inaugurated during this period, remains one of Asia's largest dams and symbolised independent India's industrial ambition.

232.label: Goa liberated from Portuguese rule
232.sublabel: Operation Vijay, Indian Army entered Goa
232.what: India launched Operation Vijay on 18 December 1961, and the Indian Army entered Goa, ending 451 years of Portuguese colonial rule. Portugal surrendered in 36 hours.
232.upsc: Tests why Nehru delayed action on Goa (non-violence principle, international pressure), the criticism from Western powers, and the contrast with India's position on other colonial territories.
232.remember: The USA and UK condemned India's military action in the UN Security Council. The Soviet Union vetoed the resolution against India. Nehru was called a hypocrite for using force after preaching non-violence. He said Goa was never a colony by choice.

233.label: Lal Bahadur Shastri became PM
233.sublabel: after Nehru's death, second PM of India
233.what: Lal Bahadur Shastri became India's second Prime Minister on 9 June 1964 after Jawaharlal Nehru died in office. He served until his sudden death in Tashkent on 11 January 1966.
233.upsc: Tests his slogan Jai Jawan Jai Kisan, leadership in the 1965 war, the Tashkent Agreement, and his death in mysterious circumstances. He remains the only PM to die outside India while in office.
233.remember: Shastri was so small in physical stature that his feet barely touched the floor when seated in Nehru's chair. He was so frugal that after his death his family was found to be in debt. His simplicity made him beloved.

234.label: Privy Purses abolished
234.sublabel: Indira Gandhi ended payments to former royals
234.what: Indira Gandhi abolished the privy purses paid to former rulers of princely states through the 26th Constitutional Amendment, ending an annual payment that had been guaranteed at the time of accession.
234.upsc: Tests the 26th Amendment, the Supreme Court's initial striking down of the Presidential Order (before the Amendment), and this as part of Indira's Garibi Hatao socialist agenda before the 1971 elections.
234.remember: The Nizam of Hyderabad received Rs. 50 lakh annually as privy purse. The Maharaja of Mysore received Rs. 26 lakh. Total annual outflow was about Rs. 5 crore. Indira abolished it partly for savings, mostly for politics.

235.label: ISRO founded
235.what: The Indian Space Research Organisation was formally established on 15 August 1969 under the Department of Atomic Energy, building on INCOSPAR which Vikram Sarabhai had founded in 1962.
235.upsc: Tests the distinction between INCOSPAR (1962) and ISRO (1969), Vikram Sarabhai as the father of Indian space programme, and the founding philosophy of using space for development.
235.remember: India's space programme was born in a church. The first rocket was launched from St. Mary Magdalene Church in Thumba, Kerala in 1963. Parts were transported on bicycles. From that church to Chandrayaan-3 took exactly 60 years.

236.label: Project Tiger launched
236.what: Project Tiger was launched on 1 April 1973 by Prime Minister Indira Gandhi to protect tigers from extinction. Nine tiger reserves were initially created across India.
236.upsc: Tests the Wildlife Protection Act 1972 which preceded it, the current number of tiger reserves, India's tiger census, and the National Tiger Conservation Authority (NTCA) established under the 2006 amendment.
236.remember: India's tiger population had fallen from an estimated 40,000 at independence to just 1,827 when Project Tiger launched. The 2022 census counted 3,167 tigers. India now hosts about 75% of the world's striking tiger population.

237.label: Indian Airlines hijacking to Lahore
237.what: The 1984 hijacking of an Indian Airlines aircraft to Lahore was one of several incidents that accelerated India's anti-terrorism legislation, eventually leading to TADA (Terrorist and Disruptive Activities Prevention Act) in 1985.
237.upsc: Tests TADA as India's first major anti-terrorism law, its controversial provisions (preventive detention, confessions to police), and the lineage from TADA to POTA to UAPA used today.
237.remember: TADA was so widely misused that it was allowed to lapse in 1995 after a Supreme Court rebuke. It was replaced by POTA in 2002 after the Parliament attack, which was itself repealed in 2004. UAPA is now the primary tool.

238.label: Mandal Commission report submitted
238.what: The Second Backward Classes Commission, chaired by B.P. Mandal, submitted its report in 1980 recommending 27% reservation in central government jobs for Other Backward Classes. It sat on a shelf for 10 years before V.P. Singh implemented it in 1990.
238.upsc: Tests the distinction between the report submission (1980) and implementation (1990), the Indra Sawhney case (1992), the 50% cap on total reservations, and the exclusion of the creamy layer.
238.remember: The report was submitted to Indira Gandhi who buried it for political reasons. V.P. Singh implemented it in 1990 knowing it would be politically explosive. It ended his government. But it permanently changed Indian electoral politics.

239.label: Narasimha Rao became PM
239.what: P.V. Narasimha Rao became Prime Minister in June 1991 leading a minority Congress government. Despite having no majority, he pushed through the most sweeping economic reforms in India's history alongside Finance Minister Manmohan Singh.
239.upsc: Tests Rao's political manoeuvring to survive as a minority PM, his role in liberalisation alongside Singh, the Look East Policy he initiated, and his handling of the Babri Masjid demolition controversy.
239.remember: Rao won a crucial confidence vote in 1993 by what critics called vote-buying. He was later charged in the JMM bribery case. A Congress PM who transformed India economically was later frozen out of the party he served. His statue at the AICC was installed only in 2023.

240.label: Pokhran-I nuclear test
240.sublabel: Smiling Buddha, India's first nuclear test
240.what: India conducted its first nuclear test codenamed Smiling Buddha at Pokhran, Rajasthan on 18 May 1974 under PM Indira Gandhi. India called it a peaceful nuclear explosion.
240.upsc: Tests the distinction from Pokhran-II (1998), the international reaction including formation of Nuclear Suppliers Group (NSG) directly in response to India's test, and India's policy of no-first-use.
240.remember: The NSG was created specifically to stop India from getting nuclear materials after this test. For 32 years it blocked India. The 2008 US-India civil nuclear deal and NSG waiver was India's ultimate diplomatic victory over the regime created to contain it.

241.label: Rajiv Gandhi assassinated
241.sublabel: by LTTE suicide bomber at Sriperumbudur
241.what: Rajiv Gandhi was assassinated on 21 May 1991 by an LTTE suicide bomber named Thenmozhi Rajaratnam at an election rally in Sriperumbudur, Tamil Nadu.
241.upsc: Tests the LTTE motive (Indian Peacekeeping Force sent to Sri Lanka by Rajiv in 1987), the IPKF controversy, and the assassination's impact on the 1991 elections which Congress subsequently won.
241.remember: The bomb was concealed in a garland. Rajiv bent to receive it. The assassin hugged him. Fourteen others died with him. The photographer Haribabu, who died in the blast, captured the final moments on film. His camera was found intact.

242.label: Atal Bihari Vajpayee became PM
242.sublabel: first non-Congress PM to complete full term
242.what: Atal Bihari Vajpayee became Prime Minister for the third time in October 1999 after the NDA coalition won elections, becoming the first non-Congress PM to serve a full five-year term.
242.upsc: Tests his key policies: Pokhran-II (1998), Lahore bus diplomacy, Kargil war, National Highways Development Project (Golden Quadrilateral), Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan, and the India-Pakistan Composite Dialogue.
242.remember: Vajpayee was a poet and orator before a politician. He was the only BJP leader Nehru praised. He introduced the Golden Quadrilateral highway project connecting the four metros, the largest highway project in Indian history at the time.

243.label: Right to Food campaign and NFSA
243.sublabel: Supreme Court orders mid-day meals in schools
243.what: The Supreme Court's 2001 interim order in the Right to Food case directed all state governments to implement the mid-day meal scheme in primary schools, a landmark order that transformed school attendance and child nutrition.
243.upsc: Tests the Right to Food case (PUCL vs Union of India), the Supreme Court's use of PILs to enforce Directive Principles, and the journey from the 2001 order to the National Food Security Act 2013.
243.remember: Before mid-day meals, many poor children came to school hungry and left after the first period. The Supreme Court fed India's children with a court order. School enrolment increased by 15-25% in states that implemented it seriously.

244.label: Demonetisation announced
244.sublabel: Rs. 500 and Rs. 1000 notes banned overnight
244.what: PM Modi announced on 8 November 2016 that Rs. 500 and Rs. 1000 currency notes would cease to be legal tender from midnight, affecting 86% of India's currency in circulation.
244.upsc: Tests the stated objectives (black money, counterfeiting, digital payments), the economic disruption, the RBI's role, GDP impact debates, and the Supreme Court's 2023 upholding of the decision.
244.remember: India ran out of new notes to replace the demonetised ones for weeks. People died waiting in queues. The informal economy, which was 90% cash-based, was devastated. The Supreme Court later upheld the decision 4-1 but called the process flawed.

245.label: Battle of Plassey
245.what: Robert Clive and the EIC defeated Nawab Siraj ud-Daulah of Bengal at Plassey on 23 June 1757 through the treachery of Mir Jafar, beginning British political control over India.
245.upsc: Tests Mir Jafar's betrayal, the Black Hole of Calcutta incident (1756) that preceded it, and the massive wealth transferred from Bengal to Britain that historians link to funding the Industrial Revolution.
245.remember: The battle lasted barely a few hours. Mir Jafar's forces, forming the bulk of the Nawab's army, simply stood aside. Clive had negotiated the betrayal weeks in advance. The real battle was fought in letters, not on the field.

246.label: Ilbert Bill controversy
246.sublabel: Indian judges to try European offenders
246.what: Viceroy Ripon's Ilbert Bill proposed allowing Indian judges to try European offenders in criminal cases. The European community in India launched a fierce campaign against it, forcing a diluted version.
246.upsc: Tests the racial hierarchy embedded in colonial law, the European opposition that revealed the limits of reform, and how the controversy radicalised Indian opinion and gave momentum to the early Congress.
246.remember: The European backlash against the Ilbert Bill shocked Indians. It made clear that the British in India would never accept legal equality with Indians. Surendranath Banerjee called it the turning point that made Indians realise they needed a political organisation. The INC was founded two years later.

247.label: Vernacular Press Act
247.what: Viceroy Lytton passed the Vernacular Press Act in 1878 to muzzle Indian language newspapers that were criticising British policies. It allowed magistrates to seize printing presses without trial.
247.upsc: Tests the press freedom issue in colonial India, the Act's repeal in 1882 by Ripon, and the contrast with how English language papers (like The Times of India) were treated differently.
247.remember: The Act was called the Gagging Act by Indians. Amrita Bazar Patrika, a Bengali paper targeted by the Act, converted to English overnight to escape its provisions. The British were outmanoeuvred by a printing press decision made in one evening.

248.label: Hunter Commission on education
248.sublabel: reviewed Wood's Despatch, shaped colonial education
248.what: The Hunter Commission of 1882 reviewed the state of education in India following Wood's Despatch of 1854. It recommended expansion of primary education and greater role for private enterprise in higher education.
248.upsc: Tests the sequence of education policy milestones: Macaulay's Minute (1835), Wood's Despatch (1854), Hunter Commission (1882), Sadler Commission (1917-19). Each shifted priorities in colonial education policy.
248.remember: Wood's Despatch is called the Magna Carta of Indian education. The Hunter Commission followed up by pointing out its failures , primary education had been neglected for 28 years while higher education expanded. The same debate continues in independent India.

249.label: Bengal famine
249.sublabel: 3 million died under Churchill's war policy
249.what: The Bengal famine of 1943 killed between 2 and 3 million people. It was caused not by crop failure but by wartime policies including diversion of food to British troops, denial of boats to prevent Japanese use, and Churchill's refusal to send aid.
249.upsc: Tests Amartya Sen's analysis that the famine was man-made not natural, the wartime context, Churchill's documented refusal to divert ships carrying food to Bengal, and this as a case study in colonial exploitation.
249.remember: Churchill reportedly said when asked about the famine: why hasn't Gandhi died yet? Food was being exported from Bengal even as people starved. The denial of denial , this famine was a policy choice, not a natural disaster.

250.label: Bal Gangadhar Tilak imprisoned
250.what: Bal Gangadhar Tilak was sentenced to six years imprisonment in Mandalay (Burma) in 1908 for writing articles deemed to encourage violence against the British after the Muzaffarpur bomb case.
250.upsc: Tests Section 124A (sedition) of the Indian Penal Code which was used against Tilak, and the public response including a six-day general strike in Bombay, the first general strike in Indian history.
250.remember: The workers of Bombay went on a six-day general strike spontaneously to protest Tilak's arrest. No one organised it. It emerged organically. It was the first time Indian workers used mass industrial action as a political tool.

251.label: Jallianwala Bagh Massacre
251.sublabel: General Dyer's order at Amritsar, Baisakhi
251.what: On 13 April 1919 (Baisakhi), General Dyer ordered troops to fire on a peaceful crowd at Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar. Official count 379 dead, actual estimates exceed 1000. Dyer blocked the only exit before ordering firing.
251.upsc: Tests the date (Baisakhi), the Hunter Commission findings, Udham Singh's revenge killing of Michael O'Dwyer in London in 1940, and Tagore returning his knighthood in protest.
251.remember: Dyer was not punished. The House of Lords praised him. The British public raised over 26,000 pounds for him. Rabindranath Tagore wrote to the Viceroy: I wish to stand shorn of all special distinctions, by the side of those of my countrymen who have been humiliated.

252.label: Nehru Report
252.what: The Nehru Report, drafted by Motilal Nehru's committee in 1928, was the first attempt by Indians to draft their own constitutional framework. It proposed Dominion Status, joint electorates, and fundamental rights.
252.upsc: Tests it as India's first self-drafted constitutional document, the rejection by Jinnah (who proposed the 14 points as counter), and Jawaharlal Nehru's opposition to Dominion Status (he wanted Purna Swaraj).
252.remember: The Nehru Report created a rift between father and son. Motilal was willing to accept Dominion Status. Jawaharlal wanted complete independence. The son won , Lahore 1929 declared Purna Swaraj. It was the only political argument a father publicly lost to his son.

253.label: Government of India Act 1858
253.sublabel: Crown Rule replaced East India Company
253.what: The Government of India Act 1858 transferred all powers of the East India Company to the British Crown following the 1857 revolt. The Governor-General became the Viceroy, representing the Crown directly.
253.upsc: Tests the institutional changes: Secretary of State for India replaced Board of Control, India Council was advisory body, and Queen Victoria's Proclamation promised equality and non-interference in religion.
253.remember: The EIC was the most powerful corporation in history. It ruled 250 million people, had its own army, and minted its own coins. In 1858 it was dissolved by a single Act of Parliament. The world's most powerful company was wound up in an afternoon.

254.label: Arms Act
254.what: Viceroy Lytton's Arms Act of 1878 prohibited Indians from possessing arms without a licence, while exempting Europeans. It was a direct response to fears of another 1857-type uprising.
254.upsc: Tests alongside the Vernacular Press Act (same year, same Viceroy) as examples of repressive Lytton-era legislation. Also tested in context of Indian resentment that fuelled the early nationalist movement.
254.remember: The Arms Act and the Vernacular Press Act were passed in the same year by the same Viceroy. One silenced Indian voices, the other disarmed Indian bodies. Both were repealed within a few years as counterproductive. But the resentment they created lasted generations.

255.label: Wavell Plan and Simla Conference
255.what: Viceroy Wavell proposed a plan in June 1945 for an Executive Council of Indians to govern, with equal Hindu-Muslim representation. The Simla Conference collapsed when Jinnah insisted the League alone represent Muslims.
255.upsc: Tests this as the last serious pre-partition unity attempt before the Cabinet Mission. Jinnah's insistence that no Congress Muslim could represent Muslims was a key sticking point that previewed partition logic.
255.remember: Gandhi pointed out that Congress had Muslim members and could not be called a Hindu party. Jinnah said only the League spoke for Muslims. This argument about who represents whom was the core of the partition debate. The Simla Conference failure made partition almost inevitable.

256.label: Royal Indian Navy Mutiny
256.what: In February 1946, sailors of the Royal Indian Navy mutinied in Bombay, raising slogans of Jai Hind and flying Congress, League and Communist flags together. The mutiny spread to 78 ships and 20 shore establishments.
256.upsc: Tests its significance as proof that the Indian military could no longer be relied upon by the British, directly accelerating the independence timeline. Also tests Congress and League leaders criticising the mutiny as premature.
256.remember: Both Gandhi and Jinnah condemned the mutiny. Sardar Patel negotiated its end by promising amnesty. The sailors felt betrayed. But the British got the message: their grip on the Indian military was gone. Independence was announced within the year.

257.label: Curzon's Bengal Partition annulled
257.sublabel: at Delhi Durbar, both sides claimed victory
257.what: King George V announced the annulment of the Bengal Partition at the Delhi Durbar of 1911, reuniting Bengal. The capital was simultaneously moved from Calcutta to Delhi. Both were presented as royal gifts.
257.upsc: Tests the two announcements at the same Durbar: Bengal reunification and capital shift to Delhi. The British framed annulment as a generous concession, not an admission of defeat. UPSC asks about both in the same question.
257.remember: The partition had been announced with great fanfare in 1905. Its annulment six years later was a humiliation for the British who had insisted it was an administrative necessity. It proved that sustained mass agitation could reverse British policy.

258.label: Champaran Satyagraha
258.sublabel: Gandhi's first civil disobedience in India
258.what: Gandhi led the Champaran Satyagraha in Bihar in 1917 against the tinkathia system, which forced indigo farmers to cultivate indigo on 3/20 of their land for European planters at fixed prices. It was Gandhi's first major civil disobedience in India.
258.upsc: Tests this as Gandhi's first application of Satyagraha on Indian soil after South Africa, the tinkathia system, and the Champaran Agrarian Act 1918 that resulted from it.
258.remember: Gandhi was ordered to leave Champaran by the British Collector. He refused. He said he recognised the legitimacy of the order but would disobey it as a matter of conscience. The Collector backed down. It was the first time British authority blinked before Gandhi.

259.label: Kheda Satyagraha
259.sublabel: Gandhi's no-tax campaign for drought-hit farmers
259.what: Gandhi led the Kheda Satyagraha in 1918 in Gujarat, where farmers demanded suspension of land revenue after crop failure. The government eventually instructed officials to recover taxes only from those who could afford to pay.
259.upsc: Tests it alongside Champaran (1917) and Ahmedabad Mill Strike (1918) as Gandhi's early Satyagrahas in India before the national Non-Cooperation Movement. Vallabhbhai Patel was Gandhi's key lieutenant in Kheda.
259.remember: Kheda was where Sardar Patel first worked with Gandhi. Patel had been a successful barrister. After Kheda, he gave it all up for the independence movement. The Iron Man of India was forged in a farmers' tax dispute in Gujarat.

260.label: Renaissance began in Florence
260.sublabel: rebirth of arts and learning in Europe
260.what: The Renaissance, a cultural and intellectual revival of classical learning, began in Florence around 1400 under the patronage of the Medici family. It spread across Europe over the next two centuries.
260.upsc: Tests the Renaissance as the intellectual precursor to the Scientific Revolution and Enlightenment, which directly influenced 18th-19th century reform movements that impacted India through British colonial education.
260.remember: The Medici family were bankers who funded artists and scholars because culture was a political tool. Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, Raphael were all Medici clients. The greatest art in history was commissioned as corporate sponsorship.

261.label: Vasco da Gama's second voyage to India
261.sublabel: arrived with warships, not trade goods
261.what: Vasco da Gama's second voyage to India in 1502 was a military expedition, not a trade mission. He bombarded Calicut, massacred Arab traders, and forced Indian rulers to accept Portuguese trading terms.
261.upsc: Tests the shift from trade to coercion in early European colonialism, the Portuguese Estado da India system, and the contrast between da Gama's first visit (welcomed) and second (terrorised).
261.remember: Da Gama captured a ship carrying 400 Muslim pilgrims returning from Mecca. He locked them inside and burned the ship. This was Europe's introduction to India's coast: as a trading partner on the first visit, as a war crime on the second.

262.label: Thirty Years War ended
262.sublabel: Peace of Westphalia, birth of nation-states
262.what: The Thirty Years War (1618-1648) was Europe's most destructive religious war. The Peace of Westphalia that ended it established the principle of state sovereignty, the foundation of the modern international order.
262.upsc: Tests the Westphalian system of sovereignty as the basis of modern international law, the UN Charter's non-interference principle, and India's consistent invocation of Westphalian sovereignty in foreign policy.
262.remember: The Westphalian principle of non-interference in internal affairs is why India cites sovereignty when criticised for Kashmir, human rights, or minority issues. Every time India says this is our internal matter it is invoking a peace treaty from 1648.

263.label: Industrial Revolution began in Britain
263.what: The Industrial Revolution began in Britain around 1760 with the mechanisation of textile production using James Watt's steam engine. It transformed Britain from an agrarian to an industrial economy within a century.
263.upsc: Tests the connection between Bengal's wealth (looted after Plassey 1757) and British industrial capital, the deindustrialisation of India under colonial rule, and why Indian handloom weavers were ruined.
263.remember: The timing is not a coincidence. Britain looted Bengal's treasury of millions after Plassey in 1757. The Industrial Revolution began three years later. Indian wealth funded British industrialisation. Indian artisans were then destroyed by the machine-made goods they helped finance.

264.label: Haitian Revolution
264.sublabel: first successful slave revolt, created Haiti
264.what: The Haitian Revolution (1791-1804) led by Toussaint Louverture and Jean-Jacques Dessalines resulted in Haiti becoming the first Black republic and the first country founded by formerly enslaved people.
264.upsc: Tests as a world history example of successful anti-colonial resistance, its influence on later independence movements, and the brutal French demand for reparations (paid until 1947) that crippled Haiti.
264.remember: France demanded Haiti pay 150 million francs as reparations for the loss of its slaves and plantations. Haiti paid this debt until 1947. A country founded by people freed from slavery spent 143 years paying their former enslavers for the crime of becoming free.

265.label: Unification of Germany
265.what: Germany was unified as the German Empire on 18 January 1871 at Versailles after Prussia's victory in the Franco-Prussian War. Otto von Bismarck became the first Chancellor of unified Germany.
265.upsc: Tests Bismarck's realpolitik, the balance of power in Europe that followed, and how German unification disturbed European stability and contributed to the alliance system that led to WWI.
265.remember: Bismarck declared the German Empire in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles, the palace of the French kings. The humiliation was deliberate. Fifty years later, France returned the favour by forcing Germany to sign the Treaty of Versailles in the same room.

266.label: Opium Wars between Britain and China
266.sublabel: forced China to accept drug trade
266.what: Britain fought two Opium Wars against China (1839-42 and 1856-60) to force China to accept imports of opium grown in British India. China was defeated and forced to open ports and cede Hong Kong.
266.upsc: Tests the triangular trade (India opium to China, Chinese tea to Britain, British goods to India), the unequal treaties, and as an example of European imperial coercion that parallels India's own experience.
266.remember: Britain fought a war to force a country to buy drugs. The opium was grown in Bengal. The profits funded the British Indian government. China's defeat in the Opium Wars began its Century of Humiliation. The trade was declared legal by British courts.

267.label: Scramble for Africa completed
267.sublabel: 90% of Africa colonised by European powers
267.what: By 1914 European powers had colonised 90% of Africa, up from 10% in 1880, following the Berlin Conference of 1884-85. Only Ethiopia and Liberia remained independent.
267.upsc: Tests the consequences of the Berlin Conference, the artificial borders that cause current African conflicts, and the comparison with Indian decolonisation as part of the global wave after WWII.
267.remember: Ethiopia defeated Italy at the Battle of Adwa in 1896, the only African country to defeat a European colonial army in the Scramble era. Emperor Haile Selassie later became a symbol of African resistance. Ethiopia's independence inspired pan-Africanism globally.

268.label: League of Nations first assembly
268.sublabel: met in Geneva, India was a founding member
268.what: The League of Nations held its first assembly in Geneva in November 1920. India was a founding member despite not being an independent nation, represented by the British Indian government.
268.upsc: Tests the anomaly of India's League membership as a non-sovereign state, the League's structure, and India's later role in shaping the UN's decolonisation agenda based on this experience.
268.remember: India sat at the League of Nations as a colony. It voted, sent delegates, and signed treaties as if it were a country. It was simultaneously a subject nation and an international actor. This created the legal precedent for India's immediate full UN membership at independence.

269.label: Bolshevik Revolution
269.what: Lenin's Bolsheviks seized power in Petrograd on 25 October 1917 (Julian calendar), overthrowing the Provisional Government. It was the first successful communist revolution.
269.upsc: Tests the two 1917 revolutions (February toppled the Tsar, October brought the Bolsheviks), Lenin's New Economic Policy, the formation of the USSR in 1922, and the Revolution's influence on Indian socialist thought.
269.remember: The revolution was announced by a single gunshot from the cruiser Aurora. Lenin had been sent back to Russia by Germany in a sealed train to destabilise the Russian war effort. It was the most consequential train journey in history.

270.label: Marshall Plan announced
270.what: US Secretary of State George Marshall announced the European Recovery Program (Marshall Plan) in June 1947, offering billions in aid to rebuild war-devastated European economies and prevent communist takeover.
270.upsc: Tests the Cold War context of the Marshall Plan, why the USSR refused it and prevented Eastern European nations from accepting, and how it created the economic division of Europe that defined the Cold War.
270.remember: The USSR called the Marshall Plan dollar imperialism. Stalin ordered Eastern European countries to refuse it. Those that accepted rebuilt into prosperous democracies within 20 years. Those that refused spent 40 years in poverty. Stalin's paranoia cost his allies a generation.

271.label: Decolonisation wave in Africa
271.what: 1960 is called the Year of Africa , 17 African nations gained independence in a single year. British PM Macmillan's Wind of Change speech acknowledged that African nationalism was an unstoppable force.
271.upsc: Tests the decolonisation wave and its link to the Non-Aligned Movement, India's role in supporting African independence through the UN, and the Bandung Conference (1955) which preceded it.
271.remember: Macmillan's Wind of Change speech was delivered in South Africa. He was telling the apartheid government to the face that colonialism was over. The South African PM did not clap. South Africa left the Commonwealth the following year.

272.label: Cuban Missile Crisis resolved
272.what: The 13-day Cuban Missile Crisis ended on 28 October 1962 when Khrushchev agreed to remove Soviet missiles from Cuba. The secret deal also included US removal of Jupiter missiles from Turkey and a no-invasion pledge on Cuba.
272.upsc: Tests the resolution mechanism, the establishment of the Moscow-Washington hotline, and the shift toward nuclear arms control that followed (Partial Test Ban Treaty 1963, NPT 1968).
272.remember: The public deal was: Soviets remove Cuba missiles, US promises not to invade Cuba. The secret deal was: US removes missiles from Turkey within 6 months. Kennedy never admitted to the Turkey part. Khrushchev kept the secret too. The world never knew how close it came.

273.label: Iran hostage crisis
273.what: Iranian revolutionaries seized the US Embassy in Tehran on 4 November 1979 and held 52 Americans hostage for 444 days following the Islamic Revolution and US support for the Shah.
273.upsc: Tests the Islamic Revolution of 1979, Khomeini's ideology, the US-Iran relationship breakdown, and the crisis as the beginning of the current Iran-US standoff that affects oil prices and regional security.
273.remember: The hostages were released on the exact minute Ronald Reagan was inaugurated as President, 20 January 1981. They had been held 444 days. Carter had spent his entire presidency trying to free them. The timing was deliberate humiliation.

274.label: Apartheid ended in South Africa
274.sublabel: Nelson Mandela freed, first free elections
274.what: South Africa held its first fully democratic elections in April 1994, ending the apartheid system. Nelson Mandela, released from prison in 1990 after 27 years, became the first Black President.
274.upsc: Tests India's historic anti-apartheid stance (India was the first country to impose sanctions on South Africa in 1946), the UN role, Mandela's influence on Indian foreign policy, and the Bharat Ratna awarded to Mandela.
274.remember: India imposed sanctions on South Africa in 1946, 48 years before the West. Mandela never forgot this. He visited India before any Western country after his release. Nelson Mandela and Jawaharlal Nehru had corresponded from their respective prisons.

275.label: Kyoto Protocol signed
275.what: The Kyoto Protocol was adopted in December 1997, the first international treaty with binding greenhouse gas reduction targets for developed nations. The USA signed but never ratified. It came into force in 2005.
275.upsc: Tests the distinction between Kyoto (binding only for developed nations) and Paris Agreement (voluntary NDCs for all), the Common But Differentiated Responsibilities principle, and India's position as a developing nation exempt from Kyoto targets.
275.remember: The US Senate voted 95-0 against ratifying Kyoto before it was even signed. The world's largest emitter at the time refused to be bound. India and China were exempt as developing nations. This fundamental unfairness drove the 20-year impasse in climate negotiations before Paris.

276.label: Iltutmish consolidated Delhi Sultanate
276.sublabel: first sultan recognised by Caliph of Baghdad
276.what: Iltutmish (r. 1211-1236) secured recognition from the Caliph of Baghdad in 1229, giving the Delhi Sultanate formal Islamic legitimacy. He is considered the real founder of the Sultanate as a stable power.
276.upsc: Tests Iltutmish's achievements: completion of Qutb Minar, introduction of silver tanka and copper jital coins, the iqta system, and his appointment of Razia Sultana as successor.
276.remember: Iltutmish was a slave who bought his own freedom, became a general, married his master's daughter, and founded a dynasty. He then appointed his daughter Razia as successor over his sons. A former slave gave the Delhi Sultanate its first female ruler.

277.label: Ghiyasuddin Balban's reign
277.sublabel: theory of divine kingship, iron fist rule
277.what: Ghiyasuddin Balban ruled the Delhi Sultanate from 1266-1287 and introduced the theory of divine kingship. He reorganised the army, established a powerful spy system, and crushed the Chahalgani (group of 40 nobles).
277.upsc: Tests his theory of kingship (king as shadow of God), the Sijda (prostration) and Paibos (kissing feet) rituals, destruction of the Chahalgani, and his administrative reforms that strengthened central authority.
277.remember: Balban never smiled in public for 40 years. He believed a king who laughed in court was showing weakness. He executed nobles who displeased him with no warning. His iron rule kept the Sultanate intact against the Mongol threat.

278.label: Muhammad bin Tughlaq shifted capital
278.what: Sultan Muhammad bin Tughlaq ordered the entire population of Delhi to move to Daulatabad (Devagiri) in the Deccan in 1327 to create a centrally located capital. He reversed the decision two years later after widespread suffering.
278.upsc: Tests this alongside the token currency experiment (1330) as examples of Tughlaq's visionary but impractical policies. Ibn Battuta documented both. UPSC asks which policies led to the Sultanate's decline.
278.remember: The story of emptying Delhi comes from dramatic chroniclers and should be handled with care. What is certain is that the policy was deeply disruptive and badly remembered.

279.label: Firuz Shah Tughlaq's reign
279.what: Firuz Shah Tughlaq (r. 1351-1388) reversed his predecessor's harsh policies, built hospitals, rest houses, and an extensive canal system in the Doab region. He also collected and translated Sanskrit texts.
279.upsc: Tests the contrast between Firuz Shah's administration and Muhammad bin Tughlaq's experiments. His canal system between the Yamuna and Sutlej is considered a major achievement in pre-colonial irrigation.
279.remember: Firuz Shah commissioned the translation of Sanskrit texts on medicine, astronomy and philosophy into Persian. He also moved two Ashokan pillars to Delhi, demonstrating curiosity about pre-Islamic Indian history that was unusual for a Muslim sultan.

280.label: Battle of Panipat (First)
280.sublabel: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi, Mughal era began
280.what: Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi at the First Battle of Panipat on 21 April 1526, using artillery and the Tulughma flanking tactics. The Mughal Empire began with this battle.
280.upsc: Tests all three Panipat battles in sequence: 1526 (Mughal Empire established), 1556 (Mughal Empire consolidated), 1761 (Maratha power broken). UPSC frequently asks which battle happened in which year.
280.remember: Babur outnumbered 8 to 1, won in a single afternoon. He used the one advantage Lodi had never faced: cannons. The noise alone panicked the war elephants who turned and crushed their own forces. Technology defeats numbers.

281.label: Humayun recaptured Delhi
281.sublabel: restored Mughal Empire after 15 years in exile
281.what: Humayun recaptured Delhi in July 1555 after 15 years of exile in Persia and Afghanistan, defeating Sikandar Sur. He died just 6 months later in an accident at his library.
281.upsc: Tests Humayun's exile (caused by Sher Shah Suri), his time at the Persian Safavid court, and the irony that he died shortly after regaining his empire. His son Akbar was left to consolidate what Humayun had recovered.
281.remember: Humayun fell down the steps of his library while descending to answer the call to prayer. He slipped on his robe and died three days later from a head injury. He spent 15 years reclaiming his empire and 6 months enjoying it.

282.label: Akbar abolished Jizya tax
282.what: Akbar abolished the Jizya tax on non-Muslims in 1564, as part of his policy of Sulh-i-kul (universal peace). This was a major departure from standard Islamic governance practice.
282.upsc: Tests Sulh-i-kul as Akbar's governing philosophy, the abolition of Jizya (reimposed by Aurangzeb in 1679), and Akbar's other tolerance measures including the Ibadat Khana debates.
282.remember: Jizya had been levied on non-Muslims in India since 712 CE. Akbar abolished it after 850 years of continuous collection. Aurangzeb reimposed it in 1679. The 115-year gap between Akbar's abolition and Aurangzeb's reimposition is the central comparison UPSC draws between the two emperors.

283.label: Guru Nanak Dev Ji born
283.sublabel: founder of Sikhism, born at Nankana Sahib
283.what: Guru Nanak Dev Ji was born on 15 April 1469 at Rai Bhoi Ki Talwandi (now Nankana Sahib in Pakistan). He founded Sikhism and composed the Japji Sahib, incorporated into the Guru Granth Sahib.
283.upsc: Tests the founding of Sikhism, the ten Gurus, the compilation of Guru Granth Sahib by Guru Arjan Dev Ji (1604), and Sikhism's synthesis of Bhakti and Sufi traditions that challenged both Hindu caste hierarchy and Muslim orthodoxy.
283.remember: Guru Nanak's first words after his spiritual awakening were: there is no Hindu, there is no Muslim. He then spent 30 years travelling to Mecca, Tibet, Sri Lanka and Baghdad. He is believed to have walked over 28,000 km to spread his message.

284.label: Guru Arjan Dev martyrdom
284.sublabel: first Sikh martyr, tortured by Jahangir
284.what: Guru Arjan Dev Ji, the fifth Sikh Guru who compiled the Adi Granth (Guru Granth Sahib), was executed by Mughal Emperor Jahangir in 1606 on charges of blessing a rebel prince.
284.upsc: Tests the first Sikh martyrdom, its role in transforming Sikhism from a pacifist to a militarised faith (Guru Hargobind subsequently wore two swords), and the beginning of Mughal-Sikh conflict.
284.remember: Before his martyrdom, Guru Arjan Dev was made to sit on a burning hot iron plate and had hot sand poured on him. He remained calm throughout. His martyrdom transformed his successor Guru Hargobind into a warrior guru who said a Sikh must be both spiritual and temporal.

285.label: Vijayanagara Empire at its peak
285.sublabel: under Krishna Deva Raya, golden age
285.what: Krishna Deva Raya (r. 1509-1529) brought the Vijayanagara Empire to its peak of power and cultural achievement. He defeated the Bahmani Sultanates, patronised Telugu and Kannada literature, and his court hosted the Ashtadiggajas poets.
285.upsc: Tests Krishna Deva Raya's military achievements, his texts Amuktamalyada and Jambavati Kalyanam, his title Andhra Bhoja, and Portuguese traveller Domingo Paes's description of Hampi as one of the best-provisioned cities in the world.
285.remember: Krishna Deva Raya defeated every kingdom he fought in 20 years of rule. Portuguese travellers described his court as rivalling anything in Europe. He wrote poetry in Telugu about a woman's devotion to Vishnu while running a vast empire. Hampi was then the world's second largest city after Beijing.

286.label: Sher Shah Suri's revenue reforms
286.sublabel: land measurement, standardised coins, postal system
286.what: During his brief rule (1540-1545), Sher Shah Suri introduced systematic land revenue based on measurement (not estimation), standardised the silver rupee, created a postal system, and built the Grand Trunk Road.
286.upsc: Tests that Todar Mal later systematised Sher Shah's revenue methods under Akbar, the silver rupee's continuity to modern day, and Sher Shah as the greatest administrator between the Mauryas and the Mughals.
286.remember: Sher Shah ruled for only 5 years. He achieved more administrative reform in 5 years than most rulers achieve in 50. He died in a gunpowder explosion at the Siege of Kalinjar. He was directing artillery when an accidental explosion killed him. Death by his own innovation.

287.label: Maratha Confederacy formed
287.sublabel: Peshwa became effective ruler after Shivaji's successors
287.what: After the deaths of Shivaji (1680) and his son Sambhaji (1689), effective Maratha power shifted to the Peshwas (prime ministers). Under Bajirao I (1720-1740) the Marathas expanded into a pan-Indian confederacy.
287.upsc: Tests the Peshwa system as the real power behind the Maratha throne, Bajirao I's military genius (never lost a battle), the five Maratha chiefs (Bhonsle, Scindia, Holkar, Gaikwad, Peshwa), and why the confederacy was weaker than a centralised state.
287.remember: Bajirao I proposed marching to Delhi and placing a Hindu emperor on the Mughal throne. He came within 60 miles of Delhi before turning back. He told his men: let us strike at the trunk, the branches will fall themselves. He died at 40, having never lost a single battle.

288.label: Nizam established Hyderabad state
288.sublabel: Asaf Jah broke from Mughal control
288.what: Asaf Jah I, the Mughal Governor of the Deccan, defeated a rival in 1724 and established the independent Hyderabad state. The Nizams ruled Hyderabad for over 200 years until 1948.
288.upsc: Tests the fragmentation of the Mughal Empire in the 18th century, the Nizam as the largest Indian princely state, and the integration of Hyderabad into India in 1948 (Operation Polo).
