288.remember: The Nizams of Hyderabad were so wealthy that they were once called the richest family in the world. The last Nizam, Mir Osman Ali Khan, kept the Jacob Diamond (one of the world's largest) as a paperweight. After integration, his wealth was frozen and he died in relative poverty.

289.label: Battle of Talikota
289.sublabel: Vijayanagara Empire destroyed by Deccan Sultanates
289.what: The Battle of Talikota in 1565 saw the combined forces of the Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Bidar and Golconda Sultanates defeat the Vijayanagara army. The capital Hampi was sacked and never rebuilt.
289.upsc: Tests the Deccan Sultanates coalition, the internal betrayal by two Muslim commanders in the Vijayanagara army, and the destruction of Hampi as the end of the last great Hindu empire of medieval India.
289.remember: Hampi took 6 months to loot and burn. A city that had taken 200 years to build was destroyed in half a year. The ruins scattered over 26 square kilometres today are what remains of what was once one of the richest cities on earth.

290.label: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaigns
290.what: Aurangzeb personally led campaigns in the Deccan from 1681 until his death in 1707, spending 26 years trying to crush the Maratha resistance. The treasury was drained, the army exhausted, and the empire began to collapse.
290.upsc: Tests this as the primary cause of Mughal decline: a 26-year war with no decisive victory, a 300-mile long camp following Aurangzeb that became a moving city of 500,000 people, and the Marathas' successful guerrilla resistance.
290.remember: Aurangzeb died in his Deccan camp at 88, having spent the last 26 years of his life at war. He wrote a final letter admitting he had come to rule the world but failed. He was buried in a simple grave with no monument, as he had requested. The greatest Mughal emperor died defeated.

291.label: Vedic period settlements on Ganges plain
291.what: By around 900 BCE, the focus of Vedic civilisation had shifted eastward from the Indus-Saraswati region to the Gangetic plains, where iron tools enabled dense forest clearance and intensive agriculture.
291.upsc: Tests the transition from Later Vedic to the Mahajanapada period, the role of iron technology in enabling the Gangetic settlements, and the geographical shift from Punjab to Bihar-UP as the centre of Indian civilisation.
291.remember: Iron tools changed everything. The Gangetic forest was too dense to clear with stone or bronze tools. Iron axes and ploughs made the Gangetic plain cultivable for the first time. The same river plain became the heartland of the Maurya, Gupta, and later Mughal empires.

292.label: Ajatashatru expanded Magadha
292.what: Ajatashatru (r. 492-460 BCE) expanded Magadha by defeating the Vajji confederacy (Vaishali) and Kashi. He built the fort of Rajgir and began Pataliputra, which became the Mauryan capital.
292.upsc: Tests Magadha's rise as the dominant Mahajanapada, the defeat of the Vajji republic (Vaishali), and the founding of Pataliputra. Both Buddha and Mahavira lived during Ajatashatru's reign.
292.remember: Ajatashatru killed his father Bimbisara to seize the throne. Both the Buddha and Mahavira were alive during his reign and he met both of them. His reign marks the point when Magadha became the dominant power that would eventually become the Maurya Empire.

293.label: Chandragupta Maurya met Alexander's successors
293.sublabel: Treaty with Seleucus, married Greek princess
293.what: Chandragupta Maurya defeated Seleucus Nicator (Alexander's general who took over eastern territories) around 305 BCE. The peace treaty involved Seleucus ceding eastern territories in exchange for 500 war elephants and a royal marriage alliance.
293.upsc: Tests the Maurya-Seleucid Treaty as India's first recorded diplomatic agreement with a Western power, Megasthenes (Seleucus's ambassador) whose Indica is a primary source for Mauryan India, and the scale of territory ceded.
293.remember: Seleucus gave Chandragupta his daughter in marriage and got 500 war elephants. He then used those elephants to defeat his rivals in the west. Chandragupta turned a military defeat into a diplomatic masterpiece. The elephants went on to decide battles across the Mediterranean.

294.label: Megasthenes visited Pataliputra
294.sublabel: wrote Indica, primary source for Mauryan India
294.what: Megasthenes, the Greek ambassador sent by Seleucus to Chandragupta's court, lived in Pataliputra and wrote the Indica, the first detailed Western account of India. The original is lost but survives in fragments quoted by later authors.
294.upsc: Tests Indica as a primary source for Mauryan administration, Megasthenes's description of Pataliputra's city administration, and his observations about the caste system, army, and agricultural practices.
294.remember: Megasthenes described Pataliputra as a city of 400,000 people with walls and 570 towers, surrounded by a moat. No city in Europe at that time approached this scale. He also noted there was no slavery in India, which puzzled his Greek readers who considered slavery natural.

295.label: Kanishka I convened Fourth Buddhist Council
295.sublabel: Kushan emperor, spread Buddhism to Central Asia
295.what: Kanishka I, the great Kushan emperor, convened the Fourth Buddhist Council in Kashmir around 100 CE to resolve disputes in Buddhist doctrine. He patronised Buddhism and spread it along the Silk Road to Central Asia and China.
295.upsc: Tests the four Buddhist councils (First: Rajgriha; Second: Vaishali; Third: Pataliputra under Ashoka; Fourth: Kashmir under Kanishka), the Mahayana-Hinayana split, and Kanishka's role in spreading Buddhism internationally.
295.remember: Kanishka's coins are the first to depict the Buddha in human form. Before Kanishka, Buddhist art showed the Buddha only through symbols (footprints, umbrella, wheel). The human image of Buddha that the world knows was created during the Kushana period.

296.label: Samudragupta's conquests
296.sublabel: Allahabad Pillar Inscription describes all India campaign
296.what: Samudragupta (r. 335-375 CE) conducted a military campaign across India described in the Allahabad Pillar Inscription by his court poet Harishena. He is called the Napoleon of India for his conquests.
296.upsc: Tests the Allahabad Pillar Inscription as a primary source, Samudragupta's policy of different treatment for different regions (annexation in the north, tribute in the south), and his title Kaviraja (king of poets).
296.remember: The Allahabad Pillar was originally an Ashokan pillar. Samudragupta had his achievements inscribed on Ashoka's pillar. Then Jahangir added his own inscription. Then the British added information about their surveys. One pillar holds 2000 years of Indian history.

297.label: Fa Hien visited India
297.what: The Chinese Buddhist pilgrim Fa Hien (Faxian) visited India from 399-414 CE during the reign of Chandragupta II, documenting Buddhism, the Gupta administration, and daily life. His account is a primary source for the Gupta period.
297.upsc: Tests Fa Hien alongside Hieun Tsang as the two key Chinese pilgrims. Fa Hien documented the Gupta Golden Age, describing a peaceful prosperous society with no capital punishment and unlocked houses.
297.remember: Fa Hien described Gupta India as a land where people left their doors unlocked and no one needed to register travel. He had walked from China through Central Asia and was astonished by the prosperity. His account is the closest thing we have to a tourist's view of ancient India.

298.label: Chalukya dynasty founded
298.what: Pulakesi I founded the Chalukya dynasty at Badami (Vatapi) in 543 CE. The Badami Chalukyas became the dominant power in the Deccan, famously defeating Harsha's northward expansion.
298.upsc: Tests Pulakesi II's defeat of Harsha's southward expansion (634 CE), the Chalukya-Pallava conflicts, and the Aihole inscription by court poet Ravikirti which describes Pulakesi II's achievements.
298.remember: Pulakesin II's success against Harsha made the Narmada an important political boundary between northern and southern spheres.

299.label: Rashtrakuta dynasty at peak
299.what: The Rashtrakuta dynasty, dominant in the Deccan from the 8th to 10th centuries, reached its cultural peak under Dantidurga and his successors. Krishna I commissioned the monolithic Kailasa Temple at Ellora around 757 CE.
299.upsc: Tests the Kailasa Temple as the world's largest monolithic rock-cut temple, carved downward from a single cliff face, and the Rashtrakutas as patrons of both Hindu and Jain art alongside their political dominance.
299.remember: The Kailasa Temple at Ellora was carved from the top down. They first cut the top of the rock, then carved downward, removing 200,000 tonnes of rock to reveal the temple. It was completed in less than 20 years. Modern engineers say they could not replicate it today.

300.label: Adi Shankaracharya's Advaita Vedanta
300.what: Adi Shankaracharya (788-820 CE) travelled across India consolidating Hindu philosophy through his Advaita Vedanta doctrine (non-dualism) and establishing four mathas at Sringeri, Puri, Dwarka and Badrinath.
300.upsc: Tests Advaita Vedanta as a philosophical system, the four mathas as organisational tools to spread Hinduism, and Shankaracharya's role in reviving Hinduism at a time when Buddhism was the dominant intellectual tradition.
300.remember: Shankaracharya died at 32. In 32 years he walked across the entire Indian subcontinent multiple times, wrote major philosophical texts, and established four institutions that still exist 1200 years later. He is considered the most influential Hindu philosopher in history.

301.label: Rajendra Chola's naval expedition
301.sublabel: conquered Sri Vijaya, expanded to Southeast Asia
301.what: Rajendra Chola launched a massive naval expedition in 1025 CE that conquered parts of the Sri Vijaya empire (Malay Peninsula and Sumatra) and established Chola suzerainty over Southeast Asian trade routes.
301.upsc: Tests Rajendra Chola's naval power as the greatest in Indian history, his title Gangaikonda (the one who brought the Ganga), and the Chola cultural influence on Southeast Asian art, religion, and political systems visible today.
301.remember: Rajendra Chola's navy crossed 2000 km of open ocean to defeat Sri Vijaya. The Hindu temples of Angkor Wat and throughout Southeast Asia trace their architectural origins to the Chola style. India's maritime legacy is carried in every Thai, Cambodian and Balinese temple.

302.label: Second Urbanisation in Gangetic plains
302.what: Around 600 BCE, the Gangetic plains saw the emergence of new urban centres, marking India's Second Urbanisation (after the Indus Valley). Cities like Varanasi, Rajgir, Vaishali, and Champa developed as centres of trade and culture.
302.upsc: Tests the distinction between the First Urbanisation (Indus Valley) and Second Urbanisation (Gangetic cities), the role of iron tools in enabling dense settlement, and the connection to the Mahajanapada period.
302.remember: Varanasi is continuously inhabited for at least 3000 years, making it one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities on earth. Mark Twain wrote: Benares is older than history, older than tradition, older even than legend, and looks twice as old as all of them put together.

303.label: Vakataka dynasty and Gupta alliance
303.sublabel: Chandragupta II's daughter married Vakataka king
303.what: The Vakataka dynasty of the Deccan formed a matrimonial alliance with the Guptas through Chandragupta II's daughter Prabhavati Gupta. This alliance extended Gupta influence into the Deccan without military conquest.
303.upsc: Tests the Gupta strategy of matrimonial alliances for expansion, the Vakataka patronage of the Ajanta cave paintings (commissioned during this period), and the Gupta-Vakataka cultural synthesis.
303.remember: The Ajanta cave paintings were commissioned by Vakataka patrons during the Gupta period. The artists who painted them created the greatest pre-Renaissance narrative painting tradition in the world. These caves were then sealed and forgotten until a British soldier rediscovered them in 1819.

304.label: Huna invasions and Gupta decline
304.sublabel: White Huns broke Gupta power in northwest
304.what: The Huna (White Hun) invasions beginning around 455 CE under Toramana and then Mihirakula devastated northwestern India and broke the Gupta Empire's hold over its northern provinces, accelerating its decline.
304.upsc: Tests the Huna invasions alongside the weak successors and the Vakataka separation as causes of Gupta decline, and that Yashodharman of Malwa eventually defeated Mihirakula, the most brutal Huna ruler.
304.remember: Mihirakula was described by contemporaries as the most cruel ruler India had ever seen. He reportedly threw elephants off cliffs to watch them die. He was eventually defeated and humiliated by a regional king. The Gupta Empire never recovered from the Huna century.

305.label: Pallava dynasty established at Kanchipuram
305.what: The Pallava dynasty established its capital at Kanchipuram around the 3rd century CE and became the dominant power in the Tamil region for 500 years, creating the Dravidian architectural style.
305.upsc: Tests the Pallava contribution to Dravidian architecture (rathas, mandapas, structural temples), the Pallava-Chalukya conflicts, and the Grantha script that the Pallavas developed, which became the parent script for Tamil, Kannada, and Southeast Asian scripts.
305.remember: Pallava-era scripts influenced writing systems across Southeast Asia. That is one of those quiet facts with huge civilisational reach.

306.label: Kalidasa wrote Meghadutam
306.what: Kalidasa composed Meghadutam (The Cloud Messenger) during the Gupta period, a lyric poem in which a yaksha exiled from his beloved asks a cloud to carry a message to her. It is considered the pinnacle of Sanskrit lyric poetry.
306.upsc: Tests Kalidasa as the greatest Sanskrit poet and playwright, the Gupta period as the Golden Age of Sanskrit literature, and specific works: Meghadutam, Raghuvamsa, Kumarasambhava, Abhijnanashakuntalam, Vikramorvasiyam.
306.remember: Meghadutam is about a cloud being asked to carry a love message 1000 km from central India to the Himalayas. The poem describes the entire Indian landscape along the cloud's route. Ancient Indians created the world's first poetic geography of their own land.

307.label: Chandrashekhar Azad's last stand
307.what: Chandrashekhar Azad, leader of the Hindustan Socialist Republican Association, died on 27 February 1931 in Alfred Park (now Azad Park), Allahabad. Surrounded by British police, he shot himself rather than be captured.
307.upsc: Tests HSRA alongside Bhagat Singh's activities, Azad's vow to never be arrested (he kept it), and his role in organising the revolutionary movement that ran parallel to Gandhi's non-violent independence struggle.
307.remember: Azad had sworn as a teenager that he would never be arrested. He kept his word by shooting himself. He was 24. He had told Bhagat Singh: the day I am arrested, consider me dead. He was never arrested.

308.label: Sarojini Naidu became Congress president
308.sublabel: first woman president of Indian National Congress
308.what: Sarojini Naidu became the first woman President of the Indian National Congress at the Kanpur session in 1925. Known as the Nightingale of India, she was also a poet and later became Governor of Uttar Pradesh.
308.upsc: Tests her as first woman INC president, her participation in Salt March, and her role as first woman Governor (of UP) in independent India. Also tested as a poet and women's rights advocate.
308.remember: Sarojini Naidu was called the Nightingale of India for her poetry but she called herself a sparrow that had been mistaken for a nightingale. She told Gandhi that it cost a lot of money to keep him in poverty, referring to the expensive arrangements made for his simple lifestyle.

309.label: Dadabhai Naoroji's Drain Theory
309.sublabel: proved British were draining India's wealth
309.what: Dadabhai Naoroji (1825-1917), called the Grand Old Man of India, presented his Drain Theory in 1867, demonstrating through statistical analysis that Britain was systematically extracting India's wealth through home charges, trade imbalances, and civil service salaries.
309.upsc: Tests the Drain Theory as the first systematic economic critique of colonialism, Naoroji's Poverty and Un-British Rule in India (1901), and his election to the British Parliament (first Indian MP, 1892).
309.remember: Naoroji was elected to the British Parliament in 1892, representing Finsbury, London. A British MP calling for Indian independence while sitting in the House of Commons was so embarrassing to the British government that Lord Salisbury called him a black man. The insult backfired enormously.

310.label: Gopal Ganesh Agarkar founded Sudharak
310.what: Gopal Ganesh Agarkar (1856-1895) founded the Marathi journal Sudharak in 1888, arguing that social reform must precede political freedom. He was Tilak's co-founder of the New English School but disagreed sharply on the priority of reform vs. independence.
310.upsc: Tests the Agarkar-Tilak debate as the defining 19th century argument in Indian public life: social reform first (Agarkar) or political freedom first (Tilak). This debate influenced Gandhi and continues in modern Indian public discourse.
310.remember: Agarkar and Tilak founded a school together, lived in the same house, and became opponents. Agarkar said: what use is independence if casteism remains? Tilak said: what use is reform without freedom? Both were right. Independent India is still having this argument.

311.label: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad
311.what: Maulana Abul Kalam Azad (1888-1958) became Congress President at 35, the youngest ever, and served twice. He fiercely opposed partition, arguing that Islam was as much at home in India as Hinduism. He became India's first Education Minister.
311.upsc: Tests his role in the Khilafat Movement, his opposition to partition alongside Gandhi, his 6-year Congress presidency (1940-1946), and his role in establishing IITs and founding the UGC as Education Minister.
311.remember: Azad predicted that partition would be a tragedy for Muslims. He said Muslims who went to Pakistan would be second class citizens in a Muslim country, while Muslims who stayed in India would be treated with suspicion. He was correct on both counts. His book India Wins Freedom was released 30 years after his death.

312.label: Annie Besant founded Home Rule League
312.what: Annie Besant (1847-1933), an Irish activist and Theosophist leader, founded the Indian Home Rule League in 1916, advocating for Indian self-governance within the British Empire. She was also the first woman Congress president (1917).
312.upsc: Tests Besant alongside Tilak (who founded a parallel Home Rule League the same year), her role as first woman Congress president, and the Home Rule Movement as a bridge between the moderate and extremist phases of Congress.
312.remember: Annie Besant was an Irish woman who became more Indian than most Indians. She learned Sanskrit, translated Hindu scriptures, and fought for Indian independence. She was interned by the British in 1917 for her activities. The internment made her a nationalist hero overnight.

313.label: C.V. Raman discovered Raman Effect
313.sublabel: first Indian to win Nobel Prize in Science
313.what: C.V. Raman discovered the Raman Effect (inelastic scattering of light) on 28 February 1928. He won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1930, becoming the first Asian to win a Nobel Prize in science.
313.upsc: Tests the Raman Effect, National Science Day observed on 28 February, and Raman's founding of the Raman Research Institute in Bangalore.
313.remember: Raman made the discovery using equipment costing just Rs. 200. He had been told he needed expensive European laboratories to do serious science. He proved them wrong. National Science Day is celebrated on 28 February every year to commemorate this moment.

314.label: Homi Bhabha established TIFR
314.what: Homi Jehangir Bhabha established the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR) in Bombay in 1945 and later founded the Bhabha Atomic Research Centre (BARC), creating the foundation for India's nuclear programme.
314.upsc: Tests the Three Stage Nuclear Power Programme Bhabha designed (thorium-based), BARC, TIFR, and his role as chairman of India's Atomic Energy Commission until his death in a plane crash in 1966.
314.remember: Bhabha designed India's nuclear programme around thorium because India has 25% of the world's thorium reserves but very little uranium. If the programme he designed is fully implemented, India's energy needs could be met for thousands of years. The third stage is still being developed.

315.label: Jayaprakash Narayan's Total Revolution
315.sublabel: JP Movement against Indira Gandhi's Emergency
315.what: Jayaprakash Narayan (JP) launched his Total Revolution movement in 1974, calling for transformation of all aspects of Indian society. His Bihar movement against corruption became the catalyst for the 1975 Emergency.
315.upsc: Tests JP's Total Revolution concept (political, social, economic, cultural, educational, moral, intellectual revolutions together), his call to the police and military to disobey unjust orders, and the formation of the Janata Party that defeated Indira in 1977.
315.remember: JP told the army and police publicly that they were not bound to follow unconstitutional orders. This was the speech that Indira Gandhi used as justification for declaring Emergency. Indira feared a repeat of 1917 Russia. JP was arrested within hours of the Emergency declaration.

316.label: B.R. Ambedkar converted to Buddhism
316.what: On 14 October 1956, B.R. Ambedkar converted to Buddhism at a public ceremony in Nagpur along with approximately 600,000 of his followers. He died six weeks later on 6 December 1956.
316.upsc: Tests the date and significance of the conversion, the Nagpur ceremony, Ambedkar's reasons (Buddhism rejected caste, was indigenous to India), and the Navayana Buddhism he practiced as distinct from traditional Buddhism.
316.remember: Ambedkar had announced he would not die a Hindu 21 years before he converted. He kept his word. He chose Buddhism because it was born in India, had no caste system, and emphasised reason over revelation. He died six weeks after the conversion, having fulfilled his final vow.

317.label: Vinoba Bhave's Bhoodan Movement
317.sublabel: land gift movement, 4 million acres donated
317.what: Vinoba Bhave launched the Bhoodan (land gift) movement in 1951 by walking through India asking landowners to voluntarily donate land to the landless. He collected pledges for over 4 million acres.
317.upsc: Tests Bhoodan as a Gandhian alternative to land reform through legislation, the Gramdan (village gift) variant, and why it ultimately failed to redistribute meaningful land despite the large acreage pledged.
317.remember: Vinoba walked 70,000 km over 13 years collecting land pledges. Much of what was pledged was unusable land. Landowners gave away their worst fields. The movement showed the limits of voluntary redistribution against structural inequality.

318.label: Ela Bhatt founded SEWA
318.what: Ela Bhatt founded the Self Employed Women's Association (SEWA) in Ahmedabad in 1972, organising women workers in the informal economy , rag pickers, street vendors, home-based workers , into a trade union.
318.upsc: Tests SEWA as India's largest trade union for informal sector workers, Ela Bhatt's contribution to microfinance and women's economic empowerment, and SEWA Bank as a pioneer of financial inclusion for poor women.
318.remember: SEWA Bank was started with Rs. 10 membership fees from rag pickers and vegetable vendors. It is now a cooperative bank with hundreds of thousands of members. The women who swept streets and sold vegetables created their own bank because no commercial bank would serve them.

319.label: M.S. Swaminathan Green Revolution
319.what: M.S. Swaminathan collaborated with Norman Borlaug to develop High Yielding Variety wheat seeds adapted to Indian conditions, launching the Green Revolution that transformed India from a food-deficit to food-surplus nation.
319.upsc: Tests Swaminathan's specific contribution (adapting Borlaug's Mexican wheat to Indian conditions), his Swaminathan Commission Report (2004-2006) on farmer welfare, and the criticism that the Green Revolution's ecological costs were not anticipated.
319.remember: Swaminathan said later in life that the Green Revolution was a mixed blessing. He had saved millions from starvation but created dependence on chemical fertilisers and depleted groundwater. He spent the second half of his career designing the Evergreen Revolution to fix what the first revolution broke.

320.label: Ratan Tata modernised Tata Group
320.sublabel: acquired Jaguar Land Rover and Corus Steel
320.what: Ratan Tata took over as chairman of the Tata Group in 1991 and transformed it from a domestic conglomerate into a global company, acquiring Tetley (2000), Corus Steel (2007), and Jaguar Land Rover (2008).
320.upsc: Tests Tata Group's global acquisitions as examples of Indian corporate internationalisation, the significance of an Indian company buying British and European icons, and Ratan Tata's role in India's economic liberalisation era.
320.remember: Tata bought Jaguar Land Rover from Ford for 2.3 billion dollars in 2008. Ford had paid 2.5 billion for it in 1989 and was desperate to sell. The company the British created to symbolise British engineering prestige is now owned by an Indian company and makes most of its profits in China.

321.label: Milkha Singh won Commonwealth gold
321.sublabel: Flying Sikh, first Indian to win at Commonwealth Games
321.what: Milkha Singh won the gold medal in the 400m at the 1958 Commonwealth Games in Cardiff, becoming the first Indian athlete to win gold at the Commonwealth Games. He survived the 1947 partition as an orphan.
321.upsc: Tests Indian sporting history, the 1960 Rome Olympics where Milkha came fourth by 0.1 seconds (still discussed as India's greatest near-miss), and his personal story from partition refugee to national hero.
321.remember: Milkha Singh watched his parents and siblings killed in the 1947 partition. He ran, literally, for his life. He channelled that trauma into athletics. Pakistani General Ayub Khan called him the Flying Sikh after he defeated Pakistan's best runner. The man who survived partition became a symbol of partition's potential.

322.label: DRDO established
322.what: The Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) was established on 1 January 1958 by integrating existing defence research institutions. It develops military technology including missiles, aircraft, and electronic warfare systems.
322.upsc: Tests DRDO's key programmes: Integrated Guided Missile Development Programme (Agni, Prithvi, Akash, Trishul, Nag), the Tejas light combat aircraft, and the ASAT test in 2019 (Mission Shakti).
322.remember: DRDO launched Mission Shakti on 27 March 2019, destroying a live Indian satellite in low earth orbit with an anti-satellite missile. India became the fourth country after USA, Russia and China to demonstrate this capability. PM Modi announced it in a national address.

323.label: Polio eradication in India
323.what: The World Health Organisation certified India polio-free on 27 March 2014, three years after the last reported case in 2011. The Pulse Polio immunisation programme, launched in 1995, was the world's largest public health campaign.
323.upsc: Tests the Pulse Polio programme, the two oral polio vaccine drops given to children under 5, India's status as one of the most difficult countries to eradicate polio due to population density, and the public health significance.
323.remember: India was considered the hardest country in the world to eradicate polio because of dense population, poor sanitation, and high birth rate. When India was certified polio-free, health officials cried. It was the public health equivalent of a moon landing.

324.label: Param supercomputer developed
324.what: India's Centre for Development of Advanced Computing (C-DAC) developed the Param 8000 supercomputer in 1991 after the USA denied India access to Cray supercomputers citing security concerns. It was one of the fastest computers in the world at the time.
324.upsc: Tests the technology denial that motivated domestic development, C-DAC's role in India's IT ecosystem, and the Param series as the foundation of India's high-performance computing capability.
324.remember: The USA denied India Cray supercomputers because it feared India would use them for nuclear weapons design. India built its own in 18 months. The technology denial that was meant to keep India weak gave India a domestic supercomputing industry. It is the recurring pattern of India's technology story.

325.label: India's first IIT established
325.what: IIT Kharagpur was established on 18 August 1951, the first of the Indian Institutes of Technology. It was built on the site of a British detention facility and modelled on MIT, with American technical assistance.
325.upsc: Tests the IIT Act 1961, the Institutes of Technology Act, the expansion to 23 IITs today, and the brain drain debate around IIT graduates emigrating. Nehru called the IITs temples of learning.
325.remember: IIT Kharagpur was built in a former colonial prison where Indian freedom fighters had been detained. The first students studied in cells where nationalists had been imprisoned. Independence had turned a prison into a university.

326.label: AIIMS established in New Delhi
326.what: All India Institute of Medical Sciences (AIIMS) was established in New Delhi in 1956 as an autonomous institution for medical education and research, created by an Act of Parliament.
326.upsc: Tests AIIMS as an Institute of National Importance, the AIIMS Act 1956, the expansion to 22 AIIMS across India, and the institution's role in producing India's medical leadership.
326.remember: AIIMS New Delhi remains the most competitive educational institution in India. More students compete for AIIMS MBBS seats than for any other course in any country. The entrance exam is harder to crack than IIT-JEE. A doctor from AIIMS is recognised globally.

327.label: Indian Ocean Tsunami
327.sublabel: deadliest natural disaster in recorded history
327.what: The Indian Ocean earthquake and tsunami on 26 December 2004 killed over 230,000 people across 14 countries. India's Andaman and Nicobar Islands, Tamil Nadu, and Kerala were severely affected.
327.upsc: Tests India's disaster management response, the Indian Ocean Tsunami Warning System established afterward, the Disaster Management Act 2005, and the NDMA (National Disaster Management Authority) created as a result.
327.remember: The tsunami was caused by the third largest earthquake ever recorded. It released energy equivalent to 23,000 Hiroshima bombs. The waves travelled 8000 km. Indian villages that had maintained ancient oral traditions warning of water receding before a wave survived. Villages that had lost these traditions did not.

328.label: Cyclone Amphan devastated Bengal
328.sublabel: strongest cyclone ever recorded in Bay of Bengal
328.what: Cyclone Amphan made landfall in West Bengal in May 2020 as an extremely severe storm and caused enormous damage in eastern India and Bangladesh.
328.upsc: Tests the IMD's warning systems, large-scale evacuation, disaster management, and the growing discussion about climate-linked cyclone intensity in the Bay of Bengal.
328.remember: India evacuated 5 million people before Amphan struck. Despite being the strongest Bay of Bengal cyclone in recorded history, deaths were under 100 due to the evacuation. In 1999 a weaker Odisha cyclone killed 10,000 with no warning system. India's investment in disaster preparedness saved millions of lives.

329.label: Digital India programme launched
329.what: The Digital India programme was launched on 1 July 2015 with the goal of transforming India into a digitally empowered society through broadband connectivity, digital literacy, and e-governance services.
329.upsc: Tests the three vision areas (digital infrastructure, governance and services on demand, digital empowerment), the BharatNet project for rural broadband, and achievements like UPI's global expansion.
329.remember: India's UPI (Unified Payments Interface) processes more digital transactions than the USA, UK, Germany, France and Australia combined. A programme launched to bring internet to villages created the world's most used digital payment system. India went from cash-only to digital-first in under a decade.

330.label: Agni-V missile tested
330.what: India successfully tested the Agni-V intercontinental ballistic missile on 19 April 2012, with a range of over 5,000 km capable of reaching most of Asia and parts of Europe. India joined the elite club of nations with ICBM capability.
330.upsc: Tests India's missile programme, strategic deterrence, and the distinction between conventional defence projects and the nuclear-capable delivery system debate.
330.remember: For UPSC, the important thing is strategic reach and deterrence, not one dramatic city-to-city time estimate.

331.label: National Rural Health Mission launched
331.what: The National Rural Health Mission (NRHM) was launched on 5 April 2005 to improve healthcare delivery in rural India through community health workers (ASHA), improved infrastructure, and flexible funding to states.
331.upsc: Tests the ASHA (Accredited Social Health Activist) programme, the district hospital strengthening, and NRHM's merger into the National Health Mission in 2013. IMR and MMR reductions are attributed partly to NRHM.
331.remember: India trained nearly 1 million women as ASHA workers across rural India. These women , most without formal education beyond Class 8 , became the frontline of India's health system. They are credited with saving hundreds of thousands of lives by improving vaccination rates and maternal care.

332.label: Aadhaar launched
332.what: The Unique Identification Authority of India (UIDAI) was established in 2009 to create Aadhaar, a 12-digit biometric identity number linked to fingerprints and iris scans. Over 1.3 billion Indians have Aadhaar numbers.
332.upsc: Tests the Aadhaar Act 2016, the Supreme Court's 2018 partial upholding of Aadhaar (constitutional but cannot be mandatory for private services), and the JAM (Jan Dhan-Aadhaar-Mobile) trinity for welfare delivery.
332.remember: Aadhaar is the world's largest biometric database. It has enrolled more people than any identification system in history. The first person to be enrolled was Ranjana Sonawane from Maharashtra in 2010. The system she helped launch eventually covered all 1.3 billion Indians.

333.label: Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan)
333.sublabel: India first Asian nation to reach Mars
333.what: ISRO's Mars Orbiter Mission entered Martian orbit on 24 September 2014, making India the first Asian nation and first in the world to succeed on a debut Mars attempt. The mission cost Rs. 450 crore, less than many Hollywood films.
333.upsc: Tests the mission's scientific objectives (study Martian atmosphere and geology), the cost efficiency of ISRO, India joining the elite club of Mars missions alongside USA, Russia and ESA, and PM Modi's announcement live on television.
333.remember: India reached Mars for Rs. 450 crore. The Hollywood film Gravity, released the same year, had a budget of Rs. 700 crore. ISRO scientists used their mobile phones as processing units to simulate trajectory. The mission operated for 7 years, far beyond its planned 6-month life.

334.label: India's COVID vaccination programme
334.what: India launched the world's largest vaccination programme on 16 January 2021, using indigenously developed Covaxin (Bharat Biotech) and licensed Covishield (Oxford-AstraZeneca manufactured by SII). India administered over 2 billion doses.
334.upsc: Tests the two vaccines (Covaxin as India's first indigenously developed COVID vaccine), CoWIN platform for digital vaccination management, the Vaccine Maitri programme that supplied vaccines to 90 countries, and the ICMR-BBT collaboration.
334.remember: India administered 25 million doses in a single day in June 2021, a world record. The CoWIN app that managed bookings was subsequently offered to other countries as a public good. India's digital vaccination infrastructure is now a model being studied globally.

335.label: India launched first weather satellite INSAT-1B
335.what: INSAT-1B, launched in 1983, was the first fully operational satellite of the Indian National Satellite System. It provided telecommunications, broadcasting, and meteorological services, transforming weather prediction across India.
335.upsc: Tests the INSAT series as India's first operational satellite system, its role in Doordarshan expansion (television reached rural India via INSAT), and the improvement in cyclone prediction that reduced coastal deaths.
335.remember: Before INSAT, India had no early warning for cyclones. After INSAT, fishermen received weather warnings and coastal communities were evacuated. The same satellite that brought Doordarshan to villages also started saving lives from storms. Technology's highest purpose is when it saves lives.

336.label: India's first mission to the Moon Chandrayaan-1
336.sublabel: confirmed water molecules on the Moon
336.what: Chandrayaan-1, launched on 22 October 2008, was India's first lunar mission. Its Moon Impact Probe became the first Indian object to touch the Moon, and the mission's instruments confirmed the presence of water molecules in the lunar soil.
336.upsc: Tests the Moon Impact Probe carrying the Indian flag, the water discovery (NASA's M3 instrument on Chandrayaan-1 made the detection), and the mission's early end due to communication loss in 2009.
336.remember: India found water on the Moon before landing there. The water discovery changed the entire calculus of lunar exploration because water means rocket fuel, oxygen, and potentially habitation. India's first Moon mission made the Moon a more useful place for all humanity.

337.label: Chandrayaan-2 launched
337.sublabel: Vikram lander failed but orbiter still operational
337.what: Chandrayaan-2 was launched on 22 July 2019. The Vikram lander lost communication during descent and crashed near the south pole on 7 September 2019. The orbiter however continues to function and has mapped the lunar surface.
337.upsc: Tests the failure analysis (braking software error in last 2.1 km), PM Modi's presence at mission control and public consolation of ISRO scientists, and how the failure led directly to the successful Chandrayaan-3.
337.remember: PM Modi was watching live when Vikram crashed. He walked to the ISRO director and hugged him saying don't worry, India is proud of you. That image, of a leader consoling scientists after failure rather than punishing them, is credited with giving ISRO the confidence to try again and succeed.

338.label: Jyotiba Phule founded Satyashodhak Samaj
338.what: Jyotirao Phule founded the Satyashodhak Samaj (Truth-Seeker's Society) in Pune in 1873, targeting Brahmanical dominance and caste hierarchy. He and his wife Savitribai Phule had already opened schools for girls and lower castes.
338.upsc: Tests Phule's Gulamgiri (Slavery), the first school for girls opened by Savitribai Phule in 1848, and Phule's connection to both Ambedkar's anti-caste movement and the Dravidian movement.
338.remember: Savitribai Phule was pelted with stones and dung on her way to teach girls every morning. She carried a spare sari to change into after reaching school. She is now on a postage stamp. The people who pelted her are forgotten.

339.label: Child Marriage Restraint Act
339.sublabel: Sarda Act, minimum marriage age set
339.what: The Child Marriage Restraint Act of 1929, also called the Sarda Act (after its sponsor Harbilas Sarda), set the minimum age of marriage at 18 for men and 14 for women. It was the first national legislation against child marriage.
339.upsc: Tests the Sarda Act alongside the Age of Consent Act 1891 as steps in legislating against child marriage, subsequent amendments (1978 raising ages to 21 and 18), and the Prohibition of Child Marriage Act 2006.
339.remember: When the Sarda Act was passed, most Indian girls were married before puberty. The average age of marriage for girls in some communities was 5-7 years. Sarda fought for the bill for years against fierce orthodox opposition. The minimum age he achieved was still 14, a compromise from the 16 he sought.

340.label: Savitribai Phule opened first girls' school
340.sublabel: Pune, first woman teacher in India
340.what: Savitribai Phule and Jyotirao Phule opened India's first school for girls in Pune on 1 January 1848. Savitribai became India's first woman teacher, defying violent opposition from those who considered women's education taboo.
340.upsc: Tests Savitribai Phule as a pioneer of women's education, the intersection of gender and caste in education reform, and her role alongside her husband in the broader Satyashodhak movement.
340.remember: Savitribai is now officially recognised as India's first woman teacher. A university is named after her. She lived in the same century as Queen Victoria but achieved something Victoria never did: she personally taught girls from the lowest castes how to read, one by one, daily, against violent opposition.

341.label: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded Servants of India Society
341.sublabel: dedicated public servants for social work
341.what: Gopal Krishna Gokhale founded the Servants of India Society in Pune in 1905, training individuals to dedicate their lives to public service by taking a vow of poverty and working on education, healthcare, and social causes.
341.upsc: Tests Gokhale as the Moderate leader who was Gandhi's political mentor, the Society as an organisational model, and his differences with Tilak on the priority of social reform versus political independence.
341.remember: Gandhi spent a year in India after returning from South Africa at Gokhale's insistence, travelling and learning before joining politics. Gokhale told him: you do not know India. Go see it first. Gandhi obeyed. The India Gandhi came to know through those travels shaped his entire political strategy.

342.label: CSDS and community development programme
342.what: India launched its Community Development Programme in October 1952, creating community development blocks across rural India to improve agriculture, education, health, and social life through trained extension workers.
342.upsc: Tests the CDP as the predecessor of Panchayati Raj, the Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957) that recommended replacing CDP with elected Panchayats, and the programme's ultimate failure to achieve participation.
342.remember: The Community Development Programme was India's first attempt at planned rural development. It failed because it relied on government officials rather than elected representatives. The Balwant Rai Mehta Committee's conclusion was simple: you cannot develop people, you can only enable them to develop themselves.

343.label: Special Marriage Act
343.what: The Special Marriage Act of 1954 enabled Indians of any religion to marry without religious conversion or ceremonies, with civil registration. It applies to all Indian citizens regardless of religion.
343.upsc: Tests the Act as enabling inter-faith marriages, its distinction from personal law marriages, the requirement for 30-day notice (subject to challenge as unconstitutional), and its relevance to debates on Uniform Civil Code.
343.remember: The Special Marriage Act is the closest India has to a Uniform Civil Code for marriage. A Hindu marrying a Muslim can use it without either converting. The 30-day public notice requirement was designed for objections but became a tool to harass inter-faith couples.

344.label: Dowry Prohibition Act
344.sublabel: made giving and taking dowry illegal
344.what: The Dowry Prohibition Act of 1961 made the giving and taking of dowry a criminal offence punishable with imprisonment. Despite the law, dowry deaths continue and the Dowry Prohibition Rules were strengthened in 1985.
344.upsc: Tests Section 498A IPC (dowry harassment), Section 304B IPC (dowry death), the 1983 amendments adding these sections, and India's statistics on dowry deaths which remain among the highest in the world.
344.remember: India records approximately 7,000 dowry deaths per year despite the law. The 1961 Act was well-intentioned but enforcement has been historically weak. The 1983 amendment which added dowry harassment as a cognisable offence was more effective but led to misuse complaints.

345.label: Maharashtra and Gujarat created
345.what: Maharashtra and Gujarat were carved out of the bilingual Bombay State on 1 May 1960 following the Samyukta Maharashtra movement. The bifurcation completed the linguistic reorganisation of western India.
345.upsc: Tests the Samyukta Maharashtra movement (1956-1960), the deaths of 105 protesters in police firing that preceded the bifurcation, and 1 May as Maharashtra Day and Gujarat Day.
345.remember: Bombay city was the contentious prize. Both Maharashtra and Gujarat claimed it. The States Reorganisation Commission of 1955 had actually recommended keeping Bombay under central administration to avoid the dispute. The demand for Bombay as Maharashtra's capital was only won after 105 people died.

346.label: Namdeo Dhasal founded Dalit Panthers
346.sublabel: militant Dalit rights movement in Maharashtra
346.what: Namdeo Dhasal founded the Dalit Panthers in Mumbai in 1972, inspired by the American Black Panthers. It was a militant response to continuing atrocities against Dalits despite constitutional protections.
346.upsc: Tests the Dalit Panthers as a response to the failure of constitutional protections to prevent Dalit atrocities, Dhasal's poetry as a vehicle for Dalit assertion, and the broader context of Ambedkar's legacy in post-independence Maharashtra.
346.remember: The Dalit Panthers' manifesto called for land redistribution, equal wages, and an end to untouchability through collective resistance, not just constitutional appeals. It represented the frustration of a generation that had waited 25 years for Ambedkar's constitutional promises to materialise.

347.label: NALSA judgment on transgender rights
347.what: The Supreme Court's NALSA vs Union of India judgment in April 2014 recognised transgender persons as a third gender and directed the government to provide reservations and rights. It was India's most significant LGBTQ rights ruling before Section 377.
347.upsc: Tests the NALSA judgment alongside the Section 377 decriminalisation (2018) as milestones in LGBTQ rights, the self-identification principle it established, and the Transgender Persons Protection of Rights Act 2019.
347.remember: The NALSA judgment was based partly on India's ancient recognition of the hijra community in Mughal court records and Hindu texts. The court said India has historically known a third gender and the Constitution cannot be read to deny them rights. History was used to advance modernity.

348.label: Section 377 decriminalised
348.sublabel: Supreme Court struck down colonial anti-gay law
348.what: The Supreme Court unanimously struck down Section 377 of the IPC on 6 September 2018, decriminalising consensual same-sex relations between adults. The law dated from 1861 and had been used to harass the LGBTQ community.
348.upsc: Tests the journey from Naz Foundation (2009 Delhi HC struck down 377) to Suresh Kumar Koushal (2013 Supreme Court reinstated 377) to Navtej Johar (2018 Supreme Court final decriminalisation).
348.remember: Section 377 was written by Lord Macaulay in 1860. Britain decriminalised homosexuality in 1967. India was still using a British Victorian-era law to criminalise its citizens 157 years after it was written, 51 years after Britain itself abandoned it.

349.label: Maternity Benefit Amendment Act
349.sublabel: paid maternity leave extended to 26 weeks
349.what: The Maternity Benefit (Amendment) Act 2017 extended paid maternity leave from 12 to 26 weeks for women working in establishments with 10 or more employees, making India's maternity leave one of the most generous in the world.
349.upsc: Tests the amendment alongside the Sexual Harassment at Workplace Act 2013 and the Maternity Benefit Act 1961 as examples of women's labour rights legislation. India's 26-week leave is compared globally.
349.remember: India's 26 weeks of paid maternity leave is more than the USA (which has zero guaranteed paid leave), more than China (18 weeks), and close to Scandinavian countries. A developing country with a stronger maternity leave policy than the world's largest economy.

350.label: Lokpal and Lokayukta Act
350.what: The Lokpal and Lokayukta Act 2013 established the Lokpal (national anti-corruption ombudsman) and mandated Lokayuktas in states. It was the result of the Anna Hazare-led Jan Lokpal movement that brought millions to the streets.
350.upsc: Tests the Anna Hazare movement (2011), the demands for an independent anti-corruption body, the difference between the demanded Jan Lokpal and the enacted Lokpal, and the first Lokpal appointment in 2019.
350.remember: Anna Hazare's fast in 2011 at Ramlila Maidan brought more people to a protest than any event since independence. The Parliament was forced to pass a resolution supporting the Jan Lokpal concept. The final law was weaker than demanded, but an institution that did not exist was created.

351.label: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act
351.sublabel: first law recognising domestic violence as a crime
351.what: The Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act 2005 was India's first comprehensive civil law addressing domestic violence, recognising physical, emotional, sexual, verbal, and economic abuse. It created a new class of Protection Officers.
351.upsc: Tests the Act alongside Section 498A IPC and the Dowry Prohibition Act as the framework of women's protection laws, the civil versus criminal remedy distinction, and the residence rights provision that keeps women in the matrimonial home.
351.remember: Before 2005, India had criminal laws against specific acts like dowry harassment, but no law that recognised the pattern of domestic violence as a whole. A wife could complain about being beaten, but the law had no concept of emotional abuse, financial control, or the repeated pattern that constitutes domestic violence.

352.label: First Amendment to Indian Constitution
352.sublabel: restricted free speech, added Ninth Schedule
352.what: The First Amendment (1951) added reasonable restrictions to free speech, inserted Articles 31A and 31B to protect land reform laws, and created the Ninth Schedule which placed certain laws beyond judicial review.
352.upsc: Tests the Ninth Schedule as one of UPSC's most tested constitutional topics , laws placed in it cannot be challenged in court. The Supreme Court in IR Coelho (2007) held that Ninth Schedule laws can still be reviewed if they violate basic structure.
352.remember: Nehru passed the First Amendment within a year of the Constitution coming into force. The Supreme Court had struck down some land reform laws. Nehru's response was to create a schedule where laws are immune from judicial review. The Constitution was amended to overrule the Supreme Court.

353.label: Supreme Court established in India
353.sublabel: replaced Federal Court, sat for first time
353.what: The Supreme Court of India was established on 28 January 1950, replacing the Federal Court of India and the Privy Council as the final court of appeal. Justice Harilal Jekisundas Kania became the first Chief Justice.
353.upsc: Tests the Supreme Court's original, appellate, and advisory jurisdiction (Article 143), the distinction from the Federal Court it replaced, and the significance of ending appeals to the Privy Council in London.
353.remember: Until 1950, an Indian's final legal appeal went to the Privy Council in London. Indian citizens were judged ultimately by British lords who had never visited India and knew nothing of Indian conditions. The Supreme Court ended 200 years of external judicial sovereignty.

354.label: Kesavananda Bharati judgment
354.what: The Supreme Court in Kesavananda Bharati vs State of Kerala (1973) held 7-6 that while Parliament can amend any part of the Constitution, it cannot destroy its basic structure. This is the most important constitutional judgment in Indian history.
354.upsc: The single most important judgment for UPSC. It saved Indian democracy from becoming a legal dictatorship.
354.remember: It was decided by just ONE vote (7-6). If one judge had changed his mind, politicians could technically have voted to end democracy or secularism tomorrow.

355.label: Menaka Gandhi judgment
355.sublabel: expanded Article 21 to include due process
355.what: The Supreme Court in Maneka Gandhi vs Union of India (1978) held that the right to life under Article 21 includes the right to live with human dignity and cannot be taken away by any arbitrary procedure. It expanded Article 21 to cover due process.
355.upsc: Tests the shift from the narrow AK Gopalan (1950) interpretation to the expansive Maneka Gandhi reading of Article 21, and how subsequent judgments used Maneka to derive rights to education, health, livelihood, and shelter from Article 21.
355.remember: The government had impounded Maneka Gandhi's passport without giving reasons. The Supreme Court said the right to travel abroad is part of personal liberty under Article 21, and any procedure curtailing it must be fair, just, and reasonable. That last phrase opened Article 21 to become the most expansive right in the Constitution.

356.label: SR Bommai judgment
356.sublabel: restricted misuse of Article 356, President's Rule
356.what: The Supreme Court in SR Bommai vs Union of India (1994) held that the imposition of President's Rule under Article 356 is subject to judicial review and that a dismissed state government must be allowed to prove its majority on the floor of the House.
356.upsc: Tests Article 356 (President's Rule), the Sarkaria Commission recommendations incorporated in the judgment, and the floor test requirement. Article 356 had been used 125 times by 1994, often for political purposes.
356.remember: President's Rule was used 90 times between 1950 and 1994, almost always to dismiss opposition-party state governments. The SR Bommai judgment made this politically harder. After 1994, President's Rule has been imposed fewer than 20 times. One Supreme Court judgment changed how India's federal system works.

357.label: Right to Property removed as Fundamental Right
357.sublabel: 44th Amendment, became legal right under Article 300A
357.what: The 44th Constitutional Amendment (1978) removed the Right to Property from the list of Fundamental Rights (Article 19 and Article 31) and made it a constitutional right under Article 300A , enforceable but not a fundamental right.
357.upsc: Tests the progression: Property as FR in original Constitution, curtailed by 1st and 4th Amendments, removed entirely by 44th Amendment. Article 300A still protects property from arbitrary acquisition without compensation.
357.remember: Property was the most litigated Fundamental Right in India's first 28 years. Zamindars used it to challenge land reform. The government used successive amendments to restrict it. Eventually Indira Gandhi removed it entirely. Property went from being among the most sacred rights to not being a fundamental right at all.

358.label: Vishaka Guidelines on sexual harassment
358.sublabel: Supreme Court created workplace law before Parliament
358.what: The Supreme Court in Vishaka vs State of Rajasthan (1997) laid down binding guidelines on prevention of sexual harassment at the workplace, filling a legislative vacuum. These guidelines had the force of law until Parliament passed the POSH Act in 2013.
358.upsc: These guidelines had the force of law until the POSH Act was finally passed in 2013.
358.remember: The Vishaka Guidelines came from a case where a woman was gang-raped for stopping a child marriage. The perpetrators were acquitted. The outrage led to a PIL. The Supreme Court, finding no law on workplace sexual harassment, simply wrote one. A court created law because Parliament had failed to.

359.label: NJAC struck down, collegium system upheld
359.sublabel: Supreme Court struck down its own replacement
359.what: The Supreme Court struck down the National Judicial Appointments Commission (NJAC) Act in 2015, which would have given the executive a role in judicial appointments. The collegium system (judges appointing judges) was upheld.
359.upsc: Tests the collegium system (no constitutional basis, evolved through Three Judges Cases), the NJAC as the government's attempt to reclaim appointment power, and the tension between judicial independence and executive accountability.
359.remember: The NJAC had been passed unanimously by Parliament and ratified by all state legislatures , the most broadly supported constitutional amendment in recent history. The Supreme Court struck it down anyway. Five judges overruled every elected representative in the country. Whether that is judicial independence or judicial overreach is the central debate.

360.label: Puttaswamy judgment: Privacy as Fundamental Right
360.what: In Justice KS Puttaswamy vs Union of India (2017), a nine-judge Supreme Court bench unanimously held that the right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21. The case arose from a challenge to the Aadhaar programme.
360.upsc: Tests the right to privacy as part of Article 21, the overruling of MP Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) which had held privacy was not a fundamental right, and the subsequent implications for data protection law.
360.remember: India spent 63 years without a constitutional right to privacy. The government had argued in court that Indians have no fundamental right to privacy and the Founding Fathers never intended one. Nine judges disagreed unanimously. The right that authoritarian governments fear most was declared fundamental.
